Clay Smear Mechanism:** - During fault slip, ductile clay/shale beds are dragged along the fault plane, forming a continuous **smear** of low permeability. - **Clay Smear Potential (CSP)** is empirically related to shale bed thickness and fault throw: $$ CSP = \sum \dfrac{t_i^2}{T_i} $$ where $t_i$ = thickness of each shale bed, $T_i$ = fault throw at that level. - High CSP ⇒ effective seal. - **Shale Smear Factor (SSF):** $$ SSF = \dfrac{T}{t_{shale}} $$ Low SSF (typically < 4) ⇒ continuous smear ⇒ good seal. **4. Classic Example:** *Niger Delta* — growth faults with extensive clay smears trap hydrocarbons in rollover anticlines. *Ankleshwar* and *Cambay Basin* fields (Gujarat) also use normal-fault trap geometry. **5. Sketch Requirements (Exam):** - Cross-section showing footwall, hanging wall, fault plane, reservoir, cap rock, and clay smear zone. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (OPTION 2 / INTERNAL CHOICE) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Module 3, Alternative]** **(a)** *Describe the importance of joints and fractures as conduits for mineralizing fluids and as hosts for vein-type ore deposits. Give at least two examples. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Joints and Fractures — Structural Significance:** - **Joints** are fractures along which there has been no appreciable shear displacement. - They form in **systematic** sets (parallel, regional stress-related) and **non-systematic** (curved, surface-related) patterns. - Tensile joints open during folding (especially at outer arcs of fold hinges). - Shear fractures develop in conjugate sets under compression. **2. Role as Conduits:** - Hydrothermal fluids (200°C–500°C) carrying dissolved metals (Au, Ag, Cu, Pb, Zn) ascend along fracture networks. - As fluids cool, depressurize, or mix with meteoric water, metals precipitate as **vein-fill minerals** (quartz, calcite, sulfides). - **Boiling, fluid mixing, and reaction with wall rock** are key precipitation mechanisms. **3. Role as Hosts:** - **Epithermal Au-Ag veins** — formed in shallow, low-T settings; e.g., *Kolar Gold Fields* (Karnataka) in Dharwar craton shear-zone veins. - **Mesothermal lode gold** — formed at depth; e.g., *Champion Reef* (Kolar), *Hutti* gold mines. - **Polymetallic Pb-Zn veins** — e.g., *Zawar* group, Rajasthan, hosted in fracture-controlled dolomite. **4. Fracture Porosity in Hydrocarbon Reservoirs:** - Tight (low matrix permeability) carbonates and basement rocks become economic reservoirs only when fractured. - Example: *La Paz Field* (Venezuela) — fractured Cretaceous basement producing oil. **5. Diagnostic Features in the Field:** - Vein thickness, orientation (azimuth, dip), alteration halos, lineation, slickenfibers. --- **(b)** *Explain the role of unconformities in the formation of mineral and hydrocarbon deposits. Discuss paleokarst and supergene enrichment with examples. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Unconformities as Traps:** - A **sub-unconformity trap** forms when a reservoir (porous sandstone or carbonate) is truncated by erosion and overlain by an impermeable shale. - Example: *Pre-Cambrian unconformity traps* of Eastern Siberia; many North Sea traps overlie the **Base Cretaceous Unconformity (BCU)**. **2. Paleokarst Reservoirs:** - Carbonate rocks dissolved below an unconformity produce **vuggy, cavernous porosity** filled later by hydrocarbons. - Example: *Dongpu Depression* (China), *Cratonic Edge Fields* of the Siberian Platform. **3. Supergene Enrichment (Mineral Deposits):** - Below an unconformity, descending meteoric water oxidizes primary sulfides (e.g., chalcopyrite → CuSO₄), then re-precipitates as **secondary sulfides (chalcocite)** in the reduced zone, **upgrading ore grade** by 2–4×. - Example: *Singhbhum Copper Belt* (Jharkhand), *Khetri Copper Belt* (Rajasthan) — major Indian Cu deposits enriched below ancient weathering surfaces. **4. Bauxite and Laterite on Unconformities:** - Tropical weathering of basalts, granites, and sedimentary rocks produces **bauxitic/lateritic caps** on unconformities. - Examples: *Odisha Bauxite* (Panchpatmali, Baphlimali), *Gujarat Bauxite* (Kutch, Jamnagar), *Andhra Pradesh Bauxite* (Andhra's Eastern Ghats). **5. Placer Accumulation on Unconformity Surfaces:** - **Beach placers** (monazite, ilmenite, zircon, garnet) accumulate as residual lags on **wave-planed unconformity surfaces**. - Example: *Chavara–Manavalakuruchi* beach placers, Kerala/Tamil Nadu coast. **6. Sketch Requirements (Exam):** - Show pre-unconformity reservoir (truncated), unconformity surface, and post-unconformity cap rock. - Show oxidized zone, enriched zone, and protore below in a supergene profile. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students frequently **omit examples**. Always cite **at least one Indian deposit** for full marks. > - Sketches must be **labeled** (fault plane, cap rock, trap, etc.) — unlabeled diagrams get only 1–2 marks. > - Do not list trap types as a *table* without *explanation* — Part B requires descriptive answers, not enumeration. > - Numerical sub-parts (apparent dip, reserve calculation) require **complete units and step-by-step substitution** for full credit. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Night Revision!)** ### Core Concepts to Retain - ☐ **Anticline** traps buoyant fluids (oil, gas); **Syncline** preserves heavy/dense deposits (coal, placer). - ☐ **Faults are dual-purpose:** conduits (in mineral exploration) and seals (in petroleum traps). - ☐ **Clay smear** and **rock juxtaposition** are the two key parameters controlling fault-seal effectiveness. - ☐ **Joints and fractures** are the primary permeability in tight reservoirs and hosts of vein-type ore deposits. - ☐ **Unconformities** are explored for sub-unconformity hydrocarbon traps, paleokarst reservoirs, and supergene-enriched mineral deposits. - ☐ **Intrusive contacts** generate skarn (Fe, Cu, W, Mo) and porphyry (Cu-Mo-Au) ore bodies. - ☐ **Salt domes** create world-class piercement traps (Gulf of Mexico, Rajasthan salt basin). ### Key Formulas (Must Memorize) - ☐ Apparent dip: $\tan(\alpha') = \sin(\beta) \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ - ☐ OIIP: $OIIP = \dfrac{\phi \cdot A \cdot T \cdot (1-S_w)}{B_{oi}}$ - ☐ Closure height: $H_c = Z_{crest} - Z_{spill}$ - ☐ Clay Smear Factor: $SSF = T / t_{shale}$ ### Must-Know Indian Examples (Frequently Asked) - ☐ **Bombay High** — anticlinal trap, offshore Mumbai. - ☐ **Ankleshwar** — normal fault trap, Cambay Basin. - ☐ **Jharia** — synclinal coal preservation. - ☐ **Kolar Gold Fields** — quartz vein in shear-zone fractures. - ☐ **Malanjkhand** — porphyry Cu-Mo at intrusive contact. - ☐ **Singhbhum / Khetri** — supergene Cu enrichment below unconformity. - ☐ **Odisha / Gujarat Bauxite** — unconformity-hosted laterite. ### Bloom's Level Tags (For Self-Assessment) - ☐ **L1 Remember:** Name the four elements of a structural trap. - ☐ **L2 Understand:** Explain why hydrocarbons accumulate at anticline crests. - ☐ **L3 Apply:** Calculate apparent dip from true dip. - ☐ **L4 Analyze:** Differentiate conduit vs. sealing fault with examples. - ☐ **L5 Evaluate:** Justify the choice of a drilling location based on structural contour data. - ☐ **L6 Create:** Design a structural exploration model for a new sedimentary basin. ### KTU 2024 Examiner Mindset - ☐ Always **define the term** before explaining it. - ☐ Always **sketch** (even a rough one) — visual answers consistently score 1–2 marks higher. - ☐ Always **state at least one Indian example** for mineral/oil/gas trap questions. - ☐ **Numerical sub-parts:** Show formula → substitution → units → final answer. - ☐ **Avoid generic statements** like "faults are important." Be specific: "Clay smear along the fault plane reduces permeability to $< 0.01$ mD, sealing the reservoir." --- > **End of Module 3 — Topic Notes** > *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme)*

topics.title: Geometric and Genetic Classification of Fractures and Faults
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET615 - STRUCTURAL GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Geometric and Genetic Classification of Fractures and Faults

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fracture?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **fracture** is a general term used in Structural Geology to describe any mechanical discontinuity or break within a rock mass. It represents a surface across which the rock has lost its cohesion.

Fractures are broadly divided into two principal categories based on the presence or absence of visible displacement:

| Sub-Type | Defining Characteristic | Typical Field Expression |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Joints** | Fractures with **no appreciable displacement** parallel to the fracture surface (mode I opening) | Polygonal crack networks in basalt, columnar joints, cooling joints in granite |
| **Faults** | Fractures along which there has been **measurable relative displacement** of the two walls | Himalayan Main Central Thrust (MCT), San Andreas Fault |

## 1.2 What is a Fault?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> A **fault** is a fracture or a zone of fractures along which the two opposite walls have moved past each other parallel to the fracture surface. The displacement is called the **slip**.

## 1.3 Geometric vs. Genetic Classification — The Core Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Geometric Classification:** *Describes* what the fault/fracture *looks like* in the field — purely descriptive parameters like dip, strike, slip direction, separation, and apparent movement.
> - **Genetic Classification:** *Explains* why the fault/fracture formed — based on the **stress regime** (tensional, compressional, or shear) responsible for the failure.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Crumpled Paper Test:**
> Take a sheet of paper and pull its ends apart (tension). It tears cleanly along a line — this is like a **Normal Fault** (extensional).
> Now push the ends together (compression). The paper buckles and a flap rides up over the other — this is a **Reverse/Thrust Fault** (compressional).
> Finally, slide the two ends past each other horizontally (shear). A tear runs roughly vertical — this is a **Strike-Slip Fault** (wrench regime).
> This is the essence of **Anderson's Theory of Faulting**, which mathematically links the principal stress axes (σ₁, σ₂, σ₃) to the fault type that forms.

## 1.5 Key Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Internal Friction Angle (φ) for typical rock:** **30°** (the angle of internal friction is the standard Mohr-Coulomb value used for crustal rocks).
- **Theoretical angle between σ₁ and a newly formed fault plane:** **30°** (i.e., fault dips ≈ 60°).
- **Theoretical angle between σ₃ and a newly formed fault plane:** **60°**.
- **Bedding/Foliation reference plane:** Used as a marker to measure **separation** when a fault cuts layered rocks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Classification of Fractures (Joints)

### 2.1.1 Systematic Joints
- **Definition:** Joints that occur in **parallel, regularly spaced sets** with a consistent orientation.
- They typically form in **regional tectonic settings** (regional stress fields) and show a uniform strike and dip.
- **Examples:** Columnar joints in basalt, sheeting joints in granite plutons.

### 2.1.2 Non-Systematic (Nonsystematic) Joints
- **Definition:** Joints that are **irregular, curved, or randomly oriented** with no consistent geometric pattern.
- Usually **locally developed** and terminate against other joints or pre-existing structures.
- **Examples:** Joints in weathered outcrops, surface cracks due to unloading.

### 2.1.3 Other Geometric Sub-Classes
- **Hinges / Hinge Fractures:** Curved fractures typical of folded terrains.
- **Pinnate Joints:** Small, feather-like joints that form at an acute angle (~30°–45°) to the main fault plane, indicating fault propagation direction.
- **Shear Joints vs. Extension Joints:**
  - *Extension (Tension) Joints:* Form perpendicular to the local least principal stress (σ₃). Clean, sharp, often filled with quartz/calcite veins.
  - *Shear Joints:* Form as conjugate pairs in response to shear stress. Less common in nature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Systematic joints reflect regional paleostress; non-systematic joints are often surficial (exogenic) and have limited structural significance.

---

## 2.2 Geometric Classification of Faults

Geometric classification uses **four parameters**:
1. **Attitude** (strike & dip of the fault plane)
2. **Slip** (actual movement of the hanging wall relative to footwall)
3. **Separation** (apparent offset of a marker bed)
4. **Sense of Movement** (dextral, sinistral, normal, reverse, etc.)

### 2.2.1 Classification Based on Slip Direction
- **Dip-Slip Fault:** Movement is **parallel to the dip direction** of the fault plane.
  - *Normal Dip-Slip:* Hanging wall moves **down** relative to the footwall.
  - *Reverse Dip-Slip:* Hanging wall moves **up** relative to the footwall.
  - *Thrust Fault:* A low-angle (dip < 30°) reverse fault.
- **Strike-Slip Fault:** Movement is **parallel to the strike** of the fault plane.
  - *Right-Lateral (Dextral):* Opposite block appears to move to the right.
  - *Left-Lateral (Sinistral):* Opposite block appears to move to the left.
- **Oblique-Slip Fault:** Has **both** a dip-slip and a strike-slip component.

### 2.2.2 Classification Based on Separation
- **Dip Separation:** Offset measured along the dip direction.
- **Strike Separation:** Offset measured along the strike direction.
- **Stratigraphic Separation:** Perpendicular distance between two displaced marker beds measured along the fault-normal direction.

### 2.2.3 Classification Based on Relative Movement
- **Hanging Wall vs. Footwall:**
  - *Hanging Wall:* The block **above** an inclined fault plane.
  - *Footwall:* The block **below** an inclined fault plane.
- **Apparent Movement vs. True Movement:**
  - *Apparent* — what is seen in any 2D cross-section.
  - *True (Net Slip)* — the **actual 3D displacement vector** along the fault plane.

---

## 2.3 Genetic Classification of Faults

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anderson's Theory of Faulting (1951):**
> The type of fault that develops in a brittle, isotropic crust is governed by the **orientation of the three principal stresses** (σ₁ > σ₂ > σ₃) and the **vertical stress** (σ_v = ρgz) acting on the rock.

| Tectonic Regime | Vertical Stress Role | Fault Type | Typical Dip |
|----------------|---------------------|------------|-------------|
| **Extensional** | σ₁ is vertical | **Normal Fault** | Steep (~60°) |
| **Compressional** | σ₃ is vertical | **Reverse / Thrust Fault** | Low to moderate (~30°) |
| **Wrench (Shear)** | σ₂ is vertical | **Strike-Slip Fault** | Vertical (~90°) |

### 2.3.1 Genetic Sub-Classes
- **Tectonic Faults:** Formed by **endogenic** forces (plate tectonics, regional stress).
  - Sub-categories: Normal, Reverse, Thrust, Strike-slip, Oblique-slip.
- **Non-Tectonic Faults:** Formed by **exogenic** processes.
  - *Gravity Faults (Slumps):* Mass wasting on slopes.
  - *Compaction Faults:* Differential sediment loading.
  - *Solution-collapse Faults:* Karst-related subsidence.
  - *Exfoliation Faults:* Unloading due to erosion of overburden.

---

## 2.4 Genetic Classification of Joints

- **Tectonic (Endogenic) Joints:**
  - *Regional Joint Sets:* Formed by far-field tectonic stresses.
  - *Release (Unloading) Joints:* Formed by removal of overburden pressure.
  - *Cooling Joints:* Formed in igneous rocks during thermal contraction.
- **Non-Tectonic (Exogenic) Joints:**
  - *Exfoliation Joints:* Sheet-like fractures due to pressure release.
  - *Columnar Joints:* Polygonal columns in basaltic lava flows.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MANDATORY:** For a 14-mark KTU problem, memorizing these equations is non-negotiable. Note that absolute values use `\vert` to preserve the markdown table.

| Symbol / Term | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---------------|---------|-------------|------|
| **Hade (H)** | $H = 90° - \delta$ | Angle between fault plane and vertical; $\delta$ = dip | degrees (°) |
| **Throw (T)** | $T = s \cdot \sin\delta$ | Vertical component of dip separation; $s$ = net slip | m |
| **Heave (He)** | $He = s \cdot \cos\delta$ | Horizontal component of dip separation | m |
| **Dip Separation (DS)** | $DS = \sqrt{T^2 + He^2}$ | Offset measured along dip direction | m |
| **Stratigraphic Separation (SS)** | $SS = T \cdot \cos\alpha + He \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Perpendicular distance between markers; $\alpha$ = dip of marker bed | m |
| **Apparent Dip ($\delta'$)** | $\tan\delta' = \tan\delta \cdot \cos\beta$ | Dip measured in a non-strike-perpendicular section; $\beta$ = angle between section and strike | degrees (°) |
| **Net Slip Magnitude (s)** | $s = \sqrt{SS_{dip}^2 + SS_{strike}^2}$ | True 3D slip vector magnitude | m |
| **Anderson's Angle (θ)** | $\theta = 45° - \phi/2$ | Angle between σ₁ and the fault plane ($\phi$ = 30° for typical rock) | degrees (°) |
| **Mohr-Coulomb Failure** | $\tau = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi$ | Shear strength; $c$ = cohesion, $\sigma_n$ = normal stress | MPa |
| **Vertical Stress** | $\sigma_v = \rho g z$ | Lithostatic pressure at depth $z$; $\rho$ ≈ 2700 kg/m³ | Pa |

---

## 2.6 Engineering / Real-World Utility

- **Tunnel Engineering:** Joints and faults govern **rock mass quality (RMR, Q-system)** and dictate support design.
- **Dam Foundations:** Fault zones with gouge/clay are **seepage-prone** and require grouting.
- **Hydrocarbon Exploration:** Faults act as **traps or conduits** for oil/gas migration.
- **Seismic Hazard Analysis:** Active faults are **mappable sources of earthquakes**; genetic classification predicts recurrence.
- **Slope Stability:** Geometric (orientation) of joints controls **planar/ wedge failures** in open-pit mines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Components of Slip (Throw, Heave, and Dip Separation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** KTU frequently asks students to compute throw and heave for a given net slip and dip angle. This is a guaranteed 3- to 7-mark question.

### 3.1.1 Given
- **Net slip (s)** = total actual displacement along the fault plane.
- **Dip of the fault (δ)** = inclination of the fault from horizontal.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the geometric model.**
Place the fault plane at an angle δ above the horizontal. The net slip vector `s` lies entirely within the fault plane, pointing in the dip direction (for a pure dip-slip fault).

**Step 2 — Resolve the slip vector into vertical and horizontal components.**

The vertical component (Throw) is the projection of `s` onto the vertical axis:

$$
T = s \cdot \sin(\delta)
$$

The horizontal component (Heave) is the projection of `s` onto the horizontal axis:

$$
He = s \cdot \cos(\delta)
$$

> **Logic:** The slip vector `s` is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are `T` (vertical) and `He` (horizontal). The angle between `s` and the horizontal is exactly the fault dip `δ`.

**Step 3 — Verify with the Pythagorean identity.**

$$
DS^2 = T^2 + He^2 = s^2 \sin^2\delta + s^2 \cos^2\delta = s^2(\sin^2\delta + \cos^2\delta)
$$

$$
DS^2 = s^2 \cdot 1
$$

$$
\boxed{DS = s}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The dip separation equals the net slip for a pure dip-slip fault — a fundamental consistency check.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hade from Dip

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Examiners often give the dip and ask for the hade, or vice-versa.

**Step 1 — Define.**
The **hade (H)** is the angle the fault plane makes with the **vertical** plane.

**Step 2 — Apply the relationship.**
Since the fault plane is inclined to the horizontal by `δ`, the remaining angle to the vertical is:

$$
H = 90° - \delta
$$

**Example:** A fault dipping 60° has a hade of $H = 90° - 60° = 30°$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Apparent Dip Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Field sections are rarely oriented perpendicular to strike. KTU problems often ask for the apparent dip when the section is oblique.

### 3.3.1 Given
- True dip of fault or bed: $\delta$
- Angle between the cross-section line and the strike of the plane: $\beta$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Visualize the geometry.**
Consider a plane striking horizontally. The line of greatest dip (true dip, $\delta$) is perpendicular to the strike. A cross-section oriented at angle $\beta$ to the strike intersects the plane along a line that is **less steep** than the true dip.

**Step 2 — Trigonometric relationship.**
Using the spherical projection or the 3D coordinate derivation:

$$
\tan(\delta') = \tan(\delta) \cdot \cos(\beta)
$$

where:
- $\delta'$ = apparent dip (always $\leq \delta$)
- $\delta$ = true dip
- $\beta$ = angle between cross-section direction and strike

**Step 3 — Boundary check.**
- When $\beta = 0°$ (section is along strike), $\cos\beta = 1$, so $\delta' = \delta$ → **no dip visible** (section runs along strike).
- When $\beta = 90°$ (section is perpendicular to strike), $\cos\beta = 0$, so $\delta' = 0°$ → **no dip visible** (section is horizontal).
- When $\beta = 45°$ (45° oblique), $\delta' < \delta$.

> **Logic:** The apparent dip is the projection of the true-dip vector onto the section plane.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Anderson's Theory of Faulting (Fault Angle)

### 3.4.1 The Mohr-Coulomb Criterion
The shear stress $\tau$ required to cause failure on a plane is:

$$
\tau = c + \sigma_n \cdot \tan(\phi)
$$

For typical rock: $c \approx 0$ (cohesionless assumption in the upper crust) and $\phi = 30°$.

### 3.4.2 Deriving the Fault Angle

For a critically stressed crust, the failure envelope is tangent to the Mohr circle. The geometry of this tangency gives the angle $\theta$ between the fault plane and the direction of $\sigma_1$:

$$
\theta = 45° - \frac{\phi}{2}
$$

Substituting $\phi = 30°$:

$$
\theta = 45° - \frac{30°}{2} = 45° - 15° = 30°
$$

The fault plane thus makes a $30°$ angle with $\sigma_1$ and a $60°$ angle with $\sigma_3$.

### 3.4.3 Resulting Fault Dips
- **Normal fault (σ₁ vertical):** The fault dips at $90° - 30° = 60°$ (steep).
- **Reverse/Thrust fault (σ₃ vertical):** The fault dips at $90° - 60° = 30°$ (shallow).
- **Strike-slip fault (σ₂ vertical):** The fault plane is **vertical** ($90°$).

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A normal fault has a dip of 60° and a net slip of 12 m. The displaced marker bed dips 20° in the same direction. Calculate (a) the throw, (b) the heave, and (c) the stratigraphic separation.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Throw (T):**

$$
T = s \cdot \sin(\delta) = 12 \cdot \sin(60°) = 12 \cdot 0.8660 = 10.39 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Heave (He):**

$$
He = s \cdot \cos(\delta) = 12 \cdot \cos(60°) = 12 \cdot 0.5000 = 6.00 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Stratigraphic Separation (SS):**
Using $\alpha$ = dip of marker bed = 20°:

$$
SS = T \cdot \cos(\alpha) + He \cdot \sin(\alpha)
$$

$$
SS = 10.39 \cdot \cos(20°) + 6.00 \cdot \sin(20°)
$$

$$
SS = 10.39 \cdot 0.9397 + 6.00 \cdot 0.3420
$$

$$
SS = 9.764 + 2.052
$$

$$
\boxed{SS \approx 11.82 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
- The **throw (10.39 m)** is the vertical separation the field geologist observes in a cliff exposure.
- The **heave (6.00 m)** is the horizontal gap the surveyor can map.
- The **stratigraphic separation (11.82 m)** is the perpendicular distance between the two limbs of the displaced bed along the fault.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Distinguishing Geometric & Genetic Terms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners *frequently* test whether students confuse "slip" with "separation."

| Term | Geometric (Descriptive)? | Genetic (Interpretive)? | Definition |
|------|--------------------------|-------------------------|------------|
| **Net Slip** | ✓ | — | Total 3D displacement along fault |
| **Dip Slip** | ✓ | — | Component of slip along dip direction |
| **Strike Slip** | ✓ | — | Component of slip along strike |
| **Dip Separation** | ✓ | — | Apparent offset of a marker measured along dip |
| **Stratigraphic Separation** | ✓ | — | Perpendicular distance between two marker beds |
| **Heave** | ✓ | — | Horizontal component of dip separation |
| **Throw** | ✓ | — | Vertical component of dip separation |
| **Hade** | ✓ | — | Angle between fault and vertical |
| **Normal Fault** | (sense of slip) | ✓ (extensional) | Hanging wall down |
| **Reverse Fault** | (sense of slip) | ✓ (compressional) | Hanging wall up |
| **Thrust Fault** | ✓ (low angle) | ✓ (compressional) | Dip < 30°, hanging wall up |
| **Right-Lateral** | ✓ | — | Sense of strike-slip movement |
| **Pinnate Joint** | ✓ | ✓ (fault-related) | Small joints oblique to main fault |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Hierarchical Classification of Fractures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FRACTURES"] --> B["JOINTS No Displacement"]
    A --> C["FAULTS With Displacement"]
    B --> B1["Systematic Joints"]
    B --> B2["Non-Systematic Joints"]
    B1 --> B1a["Tectonic Shear Joints"]
    B1 --> B1b["Tension Gash Joints"]
    B1 --> B1c["Cooling Joints"]
    B1 --> B1d["Columnar Joints"]
    B2 --> B2a["Exfoliation Joints"]
    B2 --> B2b["Surface Weathering Cracks"]
    C --> C1["Dip Slip Faults"]
    C --> C2["Strike Slip Faults"]
    C --> C3["Oblique Slip Faults"]
    C1 --> C1a["Normal Fault Hanging Wall Down"]
    C1 --> C1b["Reverse Fault Hanging Wall Up"]
    C1 --> C1c["Thrust Fault Dip less than 30"]
    C2 --> C2a["Dextral Right Lateral"]
    C2 --> C2b["Sinistral Left Lateral"]
    C3 --> C3a["Transpression"]
    C3 --> C3b["Transtension"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Genetic Classification (Anderson's Stress Regimes)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["PRINCIPAL STRESS CONFIGURATION"] --> P1["Sigma 1 Vertical Extensional"]
    P --> P2["Sigma 3 Vertical Compressional"]
    P --> P3["Sigma 2 Vertical Wrench Shear"]
    P1 --> N1["Normal Fault Dip about 60 degrees"]
    P1 --> N2["Horst and Graben Geometry"]
    P2 --> R1["Reverse Fault Dip about 30 degrees"]
    P2 --> R2["Thrust Fault Dip less than 30 degrees"]
    P2 --> R3["Nappe and Fold Thrust Belts"]
    P3 --> S1["Strike Slip Fault Nearly Vertical"]
    P3 --> S2["Dextral or Sinistral Sense"]
    P3 --> S3["Pull Apart and Push Up Structures"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Geometric Components of a Dip-Slip Fault

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HW["Hanging Wall Block Above Fault"] --> S["Net Slip Vector s"]
    S --> T["Throw Vertical Component T equals s sin delta"]
    S --> HE["Heave Horizontal Component He equals s cos delta"]
    FW["Footwall Block Below Fault"] --> S
    AN["Apparent Dip Direction"] --> ADS["Apparent Dip delta prime"]
    ADS --> EQ["tan delta prime equals tan delta cos beta"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flow — Stress → Fault Type → Field Expression

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STR["Applied Stress Field"] --> AND["Andersons Theory phi equals 30 degrees"]
    AND --> CALC["Fault Angle theta equals 45 minus phi by 2 equals 30"]
    CALC --> NOR["Normal Fault sigma 1 vertical dip 60"]
    CALC --> REV["Reverse Thrust Fault sigma 3 vertical dip 30"]
    CALC --> STRK["Strike Slip Fault sigma 2 vertical dip 90"]
    NOR --> ENG1["Tunnel Support and Slope Stability"]
    REV --> ENG2["Dam Foundation and Tunnel Crossing"]
    STRK --> ENG3["Seismic Hazard Mapping"]
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Slip vs. Separation (Reference Block Diagram)

The figure below is a block diagram conceptualizing the spatial relationship of **net slip** (true 3D displacement) and **separation** (apparent 2D offset seen on a marker bed).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP["Upper Block of Rock"] --> F1["Fault Plane Inclined Dip delta"]
    BOT["Lower Block of Rock"] --> F1
    F1 --> MK["Marker Bed Offset by Fault"]
    MK --> NET["Net Slip s True 3D Vector along Fault"]
    MK --> TH["Throw T Vertical Component"]
    MK --> HV["Heave Horizontal Component"]
    MK --> SS["Stratigraphic Separation Perpendicular to Bedding"]
    NET --> TH
    NET --> HV
    TH --> SS
    HV --> SS
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

### Question 1. Define a fault and distinguish between geometric and genetic classification.

**Model Answer:**
A fault is a fracture or a zone of fractures along which the two opposite walls have moved past each other parallel to the fracture plane. The movement is called **slip**.

- **Geometric classification** *describes* the fault in terms of measurable parameters such as dip, strike, slip direction, separation, heave, throw, and apparent movement.
- **Genetic classification** *interprets* the fault in terms of the **stress regime** responsible for its formation — extensional (normal), compressional (reverse/thrust), or wrench (strike-slip), as per Anderson's theory.

---

### Question 2. What is the difference between slip and separation in a fault? Give the geometric relationship.

**Model Answer:**
- **Slip:** The *actual* relative displacement of the two walls along the fault plane (e.g., net slip, dip slip, strike slip).
- **Separation:** The *apparent* offset of a marker bed as measured in a particular direction (e.g., dip separation, strike separation, stratigraphic separation).

**Geometric relationship:** For a pure dip-slip fault, the dip separation equals the net slip:

$$
\text{Dip Separation} = s = \sqrt{T^2 + He^2}
$$

where $T = s \cdot \sin\delta$ and $He = s \cdot \cos\delta$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern, 14 Marks Each]**

---

### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks

**A(a).** Explain in detail the **geometric classification of faults** based on slip direction and sense of movement. Add neat schematic representations (7 marks).

**A(b).** Discuss **Anderson's Theory of Faulting** and derive the relationship between the principal stresses and the resulting fault dip. Assume an internal friction angle of 30°. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — A(a): Geometric Classification

**Step 1 — Definition.**
Geometric classification is *purely descriptive* and is based on the **orientation of the fault plane** and the **direction of relative movement (slip)** between the hanging wall and footwall.

**Step 2 — Main Classes.**

1. **Dip-Slip Faults:** Slip is parallel to the dip of the fault plane.
   - **Normal Fault:** Hanging wall moves *down* relative to the footwall. Forms under **extensional** stress. Typical dip ~60°.
   - **Reverse Fault:** Hanging wall moves *up* relative to the footwall. Forms under **compressional** stress. Typical dip >30°.
   - **Thrust Fault:** A low-angle reverse fault with dip < 30°. Significant horizontal transport (e.g., Main Central Thrust, Himalayas).

2. **Strike-Slip Faults:** Slip is parallel to the strike of the fault plane.
   - **Dextral (Right-Lateral):** The opposite block appears displaced to the *right* (e.g., San Andreas Fault, California).
   - **Sinistral (Left-Lateral):** The opposite block appears displaced to the *left* (e.g., Great Sumatran Fault).

3. **Oblique-Slip Faults:** Slip has *both* dip-slip and strike-slip components. Common in **transpressional** and **transtensional** regimes (e.g., Anatolian Fault, Turkey).

**Step 3 — Subsidiary Geometric Parameters.**

| Parameter | Definition |
|-----------|------------|
| Hade | $H = 90° - \delta$, angle of fault from vertical |
| Throw | Vertical component of separation |
| Heave | Horizontal component of separation |
| Net slip | True 3D displacement vector |
| Separation | Apparent offset of a marker bed |

**Step 4 — Field Expression.**
- A normal fault forms **half-grabens** and **horsts**.
- A thrust forms **klippen** and **nappe** structures.
- A strike-slip forms **pull-apart basins** and **push-up ridges**.

#### Model Solution — A(b): Anderson's Theory of Faulting

**Step 1 — Anderson's Premise.**
Anderson (1951) proposed that the **type of fault** that develops in a homogeneous, isotropic, brittle crust depends entirely on the **orientation of the three principal stresses** (σ₁ > σ₂ > σ₃) relative to the horizontal.

**Step 2 — The Three Regimes.**

| Regime | Vertical Stress | Fault Type | Typical Dip |
|--------|----------------|------------|-------------|
| Extensional | σ₁ | Normal | 60° |
| Compressional | σ₃ | Reverse/Thrust | 30° |
| Wrench (Shear) | σ₂ | Strike-slip | 90° |

**Step 3 — Derivation of Fault Angle.**

By the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion:

$$
\tau = c + \sigma_n \tan\phi
$$

For a cohesionless rock ($c = 0$) with internal friction angle $\phi = 30°$, the geometry of the Mohr circle tangent to the failure envelope gives the fault angle $\theta$ measured from $\sigma_1$:

$$
\theta = 45° - \frac{\phi}{2} = 45° - \frac{30°}{2} = 30°
$$

**Step 4 — Resulting Fault Dips.**

- **Normal Fault** (σ₁ vertical): The fault plane is inclined at $90° - 30° = 60°$ to horizontal.
- **Reverse Fault** (σ₃ vertical): The fault plane is inclined at $90° - 60° = 30°$ to horizontal.
- **Strike-Slip Fault** (σ₂ vertical): The fault plane is **vertical** ($90°$).

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
Anderson's theory successfully predicts the **first-order geometry** of faults from the regional stress field, and remains the foundational model in modern structural geology and neotectonic studies.

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**B(a).** Classify **joints** in detail, distinguishing between systematic and non-systematic joints, and explain their genetic significance. (7 marks)

**B(b).** A reverse fault has a dip of 35° and a net slip of 25 m. The displaced marker bed dips at 25° in the *opposite* direction. Calculate: (i) the throw, (ii) the heave, (iii) the dip separation, and (iv) the stratigraphic separation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — B(a): Classification of Joints

**Step 1 — Definition.**
A **joint** is a fracture in rock along which there has been **no appreciable displacement** parallel to the fracture surface. Joints are the most common geological structures.

**Step 2 — Systematic Joints.**
- Form **parallel sets** with consistent orientation over regional scales.
- Reflect **regional tectonic stress fields**.
- Sub-classes: *Tension (Extension) Joints* and *Shear Joints*.
- **Example:** Polygonal columnar joints in the Deccan Traps; regional joint sets in the Vindhyan basin.

**Step 3 — Non-Systematic Joints.**
- **Irregular, curved, randomly oriented**.
- Locally developed; terminate against other joints.
- Often **exogenic** (weathering, unloading, biological root wedging).
- Limited structural significance; important in geotechnical engineering for slope stability.

**Step 4 — Genetic Classification.**

| Type | Origin |
|------|--------|
| **Tectonic (Endogenic)** | Regional compression/extension; release of stored elastic strain |
| **Cooling Joints** | Thermal contraction in igneous rocks (columnar, sheet) |
| **Exfoliation Joints** | Pressure release from overburden erosion |
| **Desiccation Cracks** | Drying of fine-grained sediments (not a true rock joint, but analogous) |

**Step 5 — Engineering Significance.**
- **Tunnels:** Joint orientation governs roof stability and support design.
- **Dams:** Open joints form seepage paths requiring grouting curtains.
- **Quarries:** Systematic joints control the shape and size of dimension stone blocks.

#### Model Solution — B(b): Numerical Fault Computation

**Given:**
- Reverse fault with dip $\delta = 35°$
- Net slip $s = 25$ m
- Marker bed dip $\alpha = 25°$ in the *opposite* direction

> **Important sign convention:** When the marker bed dips in the *opposite* direction to the fault, the second term in the stratigraphic separation equation uses a **negative sign**.

**Step 1 — Throw (T):**

$$
T = s \cdot \sin(\delta) = 25 \cdot \sin(35°) = 25 \cdot 0.5736 = 14.34 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Heave (He):**

$$
He = s \cdot \cos(\delta) = 25 \cdot \cos(35°) = 25 \cdot 0.8192 = 20.48 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Dip Separation (DS):**

For a pure dip-slip fault:

$$
DS = s = 25 \text{ m}
$$

**Cross-check:**

$$
DS = \sqrt{T^2 + He^2} = \sqrt{(14.34)^2 + (20.48)^2} = \sqrt{205.6 + 419.4} = \sqrt{625.0} = 25.0 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Stratigraphic Separation (SS):**
Using the modified sign convention (opposite dip → negative second term):

$$
SS = T \cdot \cos(\alpha) - He \cdot \sin(\alpha)
$$

$$
SS = 14.34 \cdot \cos(25°) - 20.48 \cdot \sin(25°)
$$

$$
SS = 14.34 \cdot 0.9063 - 20.48 \cdot 0.4226
$$

$$
SS = 12.996 - 8.655
$$

$$
\boxed{SS \approx 4.34 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Tabulated Answer.**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Throw (T) | **14.34 m** |
| Heave (He) | **20.48 m** |
| Dip Separation (DS) | **25.00 m** |
| Stratigraphic Separation (SS) | **4.34 m** |

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
The throw of 14.34 m is the vertical distance between the two limbs of the marker bed; the heave of 20.48 m is the horizontal gap; the dip separation equals the net slip (25 m) as expected for a pure dip-slip fault. The relatively small stratigraphic separation (4.34 m) occurs because the fault and the marker bed dip in opposite directions, partially canceling their geometric offset.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Red Flags):**
> 1. **Confusing "slip" with "separation":** Many students write them interchangeably. *Slip* is the **true 3D displacement**; *separation* is the **apparent 2D offset** of a marker. Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Wrong sign in the Stratigraphic Separation formula:** If the marker bed dips in the *opposite* direction to the fault, the `He·sin(α)` term must be **subtracted**, not added. Always draw the cross-section first.
> 3. **Mixing geometric and genetic terms:** A "normal fault" is *both* a geometric term (sense of slip) and a genetic term (extensional regime). Examiners expect you to specify *which* property you are referring to.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Always state the unit (m, MPa, °) in your final answer. KTU examiners reserve **½ mark** for unit omissions.
> 5. **Writing the wrong Anderson equation:** The correct fault angle is $\theta = 45° - \phi/2$ measured **from σ₁**, not from the vertical. Getting the reference direction wrong leads to a 60° vs. 30° error.
> 6. **Not labeling the diagram:** In a 7-mark question with a "neat sketch" instruction, an unlabeled diagram is worth *zero* — examiners give marks for **labels, dip arrows, and legend boxes**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- A **fracture** is any break in rock; a **fault** is a fracture with displacement; a **joint** is a fracture with no displacement.
- **Systematic joints** are parallel, regional, tectonic; **non-systematic joints** are irregular, local, often exogenic.
- **Hanging wall = block above the fault; Footwall = block below.**
- **Normal fault** = hanging wall *down* (extensional, dip ≈ 60°).
- **Reverse fault** = hanging wall *up* (compressional, dip > 30°).
- **Thrust fault** = low-angle reverse fault (dip < 30°).
- **Dextral (right-lateral)** = opposite block appears displaced *right*; **sinistral (left-lateral)** = appears displaced *left*.
- **Oblique-slip fault** has both dip-slip and strike-slip components.
- **Slip = true 3D displacement; Separation = apparent 2D offset of a marker bed.**
- **Hade** $H = 90° - \delta$.
- **Throw** $T = s \cdot \sin\delta$; **Heave** $He = s \cdot \cos\delta$.
- **Dip Separation** $DS = \sqrt{T^2 + He^2} = s$ (for pure dip-slip).
- **Stratigraphic Separation** $SS = T \cdot \cos\alpha \pm He \cdot \sin\alpha$ (sign depends on relative dip direction).
- **Apparent Dip** $\tan\delta' = \tan\delta \cdot \cos\beta$ (always $\leq$ true dip).
- **Anderson's Fault Angle** $\theta = 45° - \phi/2$ measured from σ₁; with $\phi = 30°$, $\theta = 30°$.
- **Stress-Fault Relationship:**
  - σ₁ vertical → Normal fault (dip 60°)
  - σ₃ vertical → Reverse/Thrust fault (dip 30°)
  - σ₂ vertical → Strike-slip fault (vertical)
- **Pinnate joints** form at ~30°–45° to fault planes and indicate fault slip direction.
- **Columnar joints** are a *cooling* phenomenon, not tectonic.
- **Tectonic faults** are endogenic; **gravity/compaction/solution faults** are exogenic.
- **Engineering importance:** Faults and joints control **tunnel support, dam foundations, slope stability, and seismic hazard**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Faulting on the Outcrops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET615 - STRUCTURAL GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effects of Faulting on the Outcrops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Outcrops** are the visible exposures of bedrock or strata on the Earth's surface, where the underlying rock is revealed without any cover of soil or vegetation. **Faulting** is a tectonic process involving the fracturing and displacement of rock masses along a plane of weakness, known as the **fault plane**, due to the action of tectonic stresses.

The **Effects of Faulting on Outcrops** collectively refer to all observable geological, geomorphological, and structural modifications that occur in surface rock exposures as a direct consequence of fault displacement. These effects include the lateral offset of strata, repetition or omission of beds, formation of scarps, drag folding near the fault plane, the creation of outliers and inliers, and modifications to drainage patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The effects of faulting on outcrops are studied primarily through **map analysis**, **cross-section construction**, and **strike-and-dip relationships**. Students must be able to visually identify fault effects on a geological map and mathematically compute the displacement parameters.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a freshly baked layered cake with alternating dark and light horizontal stripes. Now, take a knife and slice the cake vertically, then push the right side of the cake **downward by 2 cm** while the left side stays put. When you look at the cake from the top (the "outcrop view"):

- The dark layer that was continuous is now **broken and offset** — it appears as two separate dark stripes.
- The offset is not vertical anymore — it has a **horizontal and vertical component** when seen in 3D.
- Near the cut, the cake layers **curve slightly** (this is **drag**).
- If you slice horizontally through the cake at a certain height, you might see the **same dark layer twice** (repetition) or miss it entirely (omission).

This is exactly what happens to rock outcrops when they are faulted! The cake is the Earth's crust, the layers are sedimentary beds, and the knife cut is the fault plane.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Outcrop** | A visible exposure of bedrock on the Earth's surface |
| **Fault Scarp** | A cliff or steep slope formed directly by fault displacement |
| **Drag** | The bending of strata adjacent to a fault plane caused by frictional resistance |
| **Outlier** | An isolated patch of younger rock completely surrounded by older rocks (erosional remnant) |
| **Inlier** | An isolated patch of older rock completely surrounded by younger rocks (erosional window) |
| **Repetition** | The same stratigraphic layer appears more than once in a sequence due to faulting or folding |
| **Omission** | A stratigraphic layer is missing from a sequence due to faulting |
| **Slickensides** | Polished, striated surfaces on a fault plane indicating direction of movement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Principle:** The effects of faulting on outcrops are governed by the **angle of the fault plane (dip)**, the **direction of movement (slip)**, the **topography**, and the **orientation of strata** relative to the fault.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Effects of Faulting on Outcrops — Systematic Classification

The effects of faulting on outcrops can be classified into the following logical categories:

#### **A. Geometric/Stratigraphic Effects**

1. **Offset (Displacement) of Beds**
   - Strata that were once continuous become physically separated along the fault trace.
   - The amount of offset depends on the **throw**, **heave**, and **slip** of the fault.
   - Visible as lateral shift of beds when traced across a fault line on a map.

2. **Repetition of Strata**
   - Occurs predominantly in **reverse faults** (or in normal faults with strata dipping toward the downthrown block).
   - The same bed appears twice in a vertical sequence on either side of the fault.
   - On a map, the outcrop width of a bed may **increase abnormally**.

3. **Omission of Strata**
   - Occurs predominantly in **normal faults** (or in reverse faults with strata dipping toward the upthrown block).
   - A bed disappears from the sequence because the downthrown block drops a younger layer against an older layer.
   - On a map, the outcrop width of a bed may **decrease abnormally** or vanish entirely.

#### **B. Deformational Effects**

4. **Drag Folding (Drag of Strata)**
   - Beds adjacent to the fault plane are bent or curved due to frictional drag during movement.
   - The direction of curvature indicates the **sense of slip** (whether the block moved up or down).
   - The curvature always **converges toward the fault plane** in the direction of relative movement of the opposite block.

5. **Slickensides and Slickenlines**
   - Polished, grooved surfaces on the fault plane.
   - Indicate the **direction of relative movement** of the fault blocks.

6. **Fault Breccia and Gouge**
   - Crushed, fragmented rock material (breccia) and clay-like powder (gouge) along the fault zone.
   - Indicates the **intensity and recency** of movement.

#### **C. Geomorphological Effects**

7. **Fault Scarp**
   - A linear cliff or steep slope on the landscape directly produced by fault displacement.
   - **Primary scarp**: formed by recent faulting, exposing fresh rock.
   - **Fault-line scarp**: formed by differential erosion of the faulted zone long after faulting.

8. **Offset Drainage**
   - Streams, rivers, and valleys are laterally displaced by faulting.
   - Right-lateral strike-slip faults create characteristic **right-stepping** stream offsets.
   - Important indicator of **strike-slip faulting** on aerial photographs and maps.

9. **Sag Ponds and Tectonic Valleys**
   - Depressions formed by extensional faulting (grabens) or by compression.
   - Often filled with water to form linear lakes.

10. **Triangular Facets**
    - Triangular-shaped erosional remnants along mountain fronts bounded by normal faults.
    - Indicate **active extensional tectonics**.

#### **D. Erosional/Map Pattern Effects**

11. **Outliers**
    - Formed when a resistant bed on the downthrown block is eroded, leaving an isolated patch surrounded by older rock.
    - Common in **horizontal or gently dipping strata** crossed by normal faults.

12. **Inliers**
    - Formed when erosion breaches a resistant capping layer on the upthrown block, exposing older rocks in a window.
    - Common where the upthrown block exposes deeper stratigraphy.

13. **Effect on Outcrop Width**
    - Outcrop width on a map is a function of **bed thickness**, **dip angle**, **slope angle**, and **slope direction**.
    - Faulting can dramatically alter the apparent outcrop width, especially in dipping strata.

14. **Change in Apparent Dip**
    - The fault plane itself introduces a new structural element that can mislead structural interpretations.
    - Cross-sections must be carefully drawn perpendicular to the fault strike to avoid apparent dip confusion.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the essential mathematical relationships used in KTU problems on faulting effects:

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| $\text{True Dip} = \arctan(\tan(\text{Apparent Dip}) \times \cos(\theta))$ | Converts apparent dip to true dip | $\theta$ = angle between true dip direction and apparent dip direction |
| $\text{Apparent Dip} = \arctan(\tan(\text{True Dip}) \times \cos(\theta))$ | Converts true dip to apparent dip | $\theta$ = angle between two directions |
| $\text{Slip} = \sqrt{\text{Throw}^2 + \text{Heave}^2}$ | Total displacement along fault | Slip (m), Throw (m), Heave (m) |
| $\text{Throw} = \text{Slip} \times \sin(\text{Fault Dip})$ | Vertical component of slip | Fault Dip (degrees) |
| $\text{Heave} = \text{Slip} \times \cos(\text{Fault Dip})$ | Horizontal component of slip (perpendicular to fault strike) | Fault Dip (degrees) |
| $\text{Outcrop Width (V)} = \text{Thickness} / \sin(\text{Dip})$ | Width measured vertically in dip direction | Thickness (m) |
| $\text{Outcrop Width (H)} = \text{Thickness} / \tan(\text{Dip})$ | Width on flat horizontal topography | Thickness (m) |
| $\text{Vertical Exaggeration (VE)} = \frac{\text{Vertical Scale}}{\text{Horizontal Scale}}$ | Ratio of scales on a cross-section | Dimensionless |
| $\text{Apparent Thickness} = \text{True Thickness} \times \cos(\text{Dip})$ | Thickness measured in any non-dip section | True Thickness (m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Students often confuse **Separation**, **Slip**, **Throw**, and **Heave**.
> - **Slip** = Total relative movement of two formerly adjacent points along the fault.
> - **Separation** = Distance between displaced beds measured along a specific reference line.
> - **Throw** = Vertical component of separation.
> - **Heave** = Horizontal component of separation (perpendicular to fault strike).

### 2.3 Engineering and Field Utility

The study of faulting effects on outcrops has direct applications in:

1. **Civil Engineering**: Tunnel alignment, dam foundation stability, road cut design across fault zones.
2. **Hydrogeology**: Fault zones often act as **aquifers** (when fractured) or **barriers** (when filled with gouge).
3. **Mineral Exploration**: Fault zones control the localization of **hydrothermal ore deposits** (e.g., gold, copper).
4. **Seismic Hazard Assessment**: Surface fault rupture studies inform building codes and earthquake-resistant design.
5. **Petroleum Geology**: Faults can act as **traps** for hydrocarbon accumulation.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: True Dip from Apparent Dip

The relationship between true dip and apparent dip is fundamental in structural geology. Consider a planar geological surface (bed or fault) with a true dip $\delta$ measured in the true dip direction. An apparent dip $\delta_a$ is observed in any other vertical section that makes an angle $\theta$ with the true dip direction.

**Geometric Setup:**

Let the plane have a unit normal vector $\hat{n}$ in 3D space. The true dip direction is the steepest descent line on the plane, and the apparent dip is the slope of the plane's intersection with a vertical plane oriented at angle $\theta$ from the true dip direction.

**Step 1: Express the true dip vector.**

The true dip vector lies in the vertical plane containing the true dip direction. It has a horizontal component and a vertical component:

$$\vec{v}_{true} = (\cos\delta, 0, \sin\delta)$$

where $\delta$ is the true dip, and the vector lies in the $xz$-plane (with $z$ vertical).

**Step 2: Rotate the vertical plane by angle $\theta$.**

The apparent dip is measured in a vertical plane that makes an angle $\theta$ with the true dip direction. The horizontal projection of the apparent dip direction is rotated by $\theta$ from the true dip horizontal projection.

**Step 3: Compute the slope of the line of intersection.**

The slope of the line of intersection between the dipping plane and the rotated vertical plane gives the apparent dip. The trigonometric relationship yields:

$$\tan\delta_a = \tan\delta \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 4: Solve for true dip:**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{\tan\delta_a}{\cos\theta}$$

$$\delta = \arctan\left(\frac{\tan\delta_a}{\cos\theta}\right)$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.**

Given: Apparent dip $\delta_a = 25°$ in a section that makes $\theta = 40°$ with the true dip direction.

$$\tan\delta = \frac{\tan 25°}{\cos 40°} = \frac{0.4663}{0.7660} = 0.6087$$

$$\delta = \arctan(0.6087) = 31.32°$$

**Interpretation:** The true dip of the bed is approximately **31.3°**, greater than the apparent dip, as expected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The true dip is always **greater than or equal to** the apparent dip, with equality occurring only when $\theta = 0$ (i.e., when measuring in the true dip direction).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Slip Decomposition into Throw and Heave

When a fault displaces rock, the total slip can be resolved into vertical and horizontal components.

**Step 1: Define the fault geometry.**

Let the fault plane dip at an angle $\phi$ from the horizontal. The slip vector lies within the fault plane, with magnitude $S$ (total slip).

**Step 2: Set up the coordinate system.**

Let the horizontal axis be perpendicular to the fault strike (pointing away from the fault) and the vertical axis be positive upward. The fault plane makes angle $\phi$ with the horizontal.

**Step 3: Resolve the slip vector.**

The slip vector, when projected:
- **Vertical projection (Throw):** $T = S \cdot \sin\phi$
- **Horizontal projection (Heave):** $H = S \cdot \cos\phi$

**Step 4: Express total slip in terms of components.**

$$S = \sqrt{T^2 + H^2}$$

$$S^2 = (S\sin\phi)^2 + (S\cos\phi)^2 = S^2(\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi) = S^2$$

This confirms the decomposition is consistent.

**Step 5: Numerical example.**

Given: Fault dip $\phi = 60°$, throw $T = 45$ m. Find the heave and total slip.

$$S = \frac{T}{\sin\phi} = \frac{45}{\sin 60°} = \frac{45}{0.8660} = 51.96 \text{ m}$$

$$H = S \cdot \cos\phi = 51.96 \times \cos 60° = 51.96 \times 0.5 = 25.98 \text{ m}$$

**Verification:** $\sqrt{45^2 + 25.98^2} = \sqrt{2025 + 675} = \sqrt{2700} = 51.96$ m ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Outcrop Width on a Geological Map

The width of an outcrop on a map depends on the bed thickness, dip, and topographic slope.

**Case 1: Flat topography (horizontal ground surface)**

The outcrop width $W$ is the horizontal distance between the upper and lower contacts of a bed:

$$W = \frac{t}{\tan\delta}$$

where $t$ is the true bed thickness and $\delta$ is the true dip.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Consider a bed of thickness $t$ dipping at angle $\delta$. The upper and lower contacts are separated by a vertical distance $t$ in the dip direction. The horizontal distance (which is what we measure on a flat map) is:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{\text{vertical}}{\text{horizontal}} = \frac{t}{W}$$

Solving for $W$:

$$W = \frac{t}{\tan\delta}$$

**Numerical example:**

Given: Bed thickness $t = 30$ m, dip $\delta = 45°$. Find outcrop width on flat topography.

$$W = \frac{30}{\tan 45°} = \frac{30}{1} = 30 \text{ m}$$

For a gentler dip of $20°$:

$$W = \frac{30}{\tan 20°} = \frac{30}{0.3640} = 82.42 \text{ m}$$

**Interpretation:** As dip decreases, outcrop width increases dramatically. A gently dipping bed has a very wide outcrop on flat terrain.

**Case 2: Sloping topography**

When the topography has a slope angle $\alpha$ (positive for slope in same direction as dip, negative for opposite):

$$W = \frac{t}{\sin(\delta - \alpha)}$$

(for slope in same direction as dip, where $\alpha < \delta$)

**Effect of faulting on outcrop width:**

If a normal fault cuts dipping beds, the downthrown block displaces beds downward, effectively **repeating** or **omitting** part of the stratigraphic section. The map outcrop width of a specific bed will change across the fault depending on:

1. The dip direction of the bed relative to the fault dip.
2. The amount of throw on the fault.
3. Whether the fault is normal or reverse.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Fault Type on Outcrops

| Fault Type | Beds Dip Toward Fault (Downthrown) | Beds Dip Away From Fault (Downthrown) | Repetition or Omission? | Drag Direction |
|------------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **Normal Fault** | Omission | Repetition | Depends on geometry | Curved toward fault on downthrown side |
| **Reverse Fault** | Repetition | Omission | Depends on geometry | Curved toward fault on upthrown side |
| **Strike-Slip Fault** | Lateral offset only | Lateral offset only | No repetition/omission (in pure strike-slip) | Curved in horizontal sense |
| **Horizontal Beds + Normal Fault** | May form outlier on downthrown side | May form inlier on upthrown side | Stratigraphic gap | Subtle drag |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:**
> - **Normal Fault + Beds dipping toward fault** → **Omission** of strata on the downthrown side.
> - **Normal Fault + Beds dipping away from fault** → **Repetition** of strata on the downthrown side.
> - **Reverse Fault + Beds dipping toward fault** → **Repetition** of strata on the upthrown side.
> - **Reverse Fault + Beds dipping away from fault** → **Omission** of strata on the upthrown side.

---

### 3.5 Worked Problem: Identifying Fault Effect on a Map

**Problem Statement:**

A geological map shows three beds: A (oldest), B (middle), C (youngest), in normal stratigraphic order. A normal fault with a throw of 50 m strikes E-W and dips 60°S. The beds strike N-S and dip 30°E. Determine the effect of faulting on the outcrop pattern.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Establish geometry.**

The fault strikes E-W and dips 60°S. This means the fault plane goes downward to the south. The southern block is the **downthrown block**, and the northern block is the **upthrown block**.

**Step 2: Determine relative dip directions.**

Beds strike N-S and dip 30°E. The fault dips S. Therefore, the beds are dipping **perpendicular** to the fault strike, with beds dipping in a direction that is **90° from the fault dip direction**.

**Step 3: Analyze repetition/omission.**

Since the beds are dipping perpendicular to the fault strike, this is a classic **dip-slip fault** scenario. On the downthrown (southern) block:
- Beds that were at the surface on the upthrown block are now buried 50 m deeper.
- Younger beds (C) on the downthrown side now appear adjacent to older beds (A, B) on the upthrown side at the same topographic level.
- This results in **omission** of the youngest part of the section on the downthrown side.

**Step 4: Calculate outcrop width change.**

Outcrop width on flat topography:
$$W = \frac{t}{\tan 30°} = \frac{t}{0.5774} = 1.732 \, t$$

After faulting, the apparent outcrop width on the downthrown side will appear **larger** for individual beds because the same bed is now spread over a wider map area due to the throw.

**Step 5: Draw the cross-section (conceptual).**

On the cross-section perpendicular to the fault:
- Upthrown side: shows beds A, B, C in normal order.
- Downthrown side: shows beds A, B, C in normal order, but offset 50 m vertically downward.
- At the fault contact: bed C on the downthrown side touches bed A on the upthrown side (omission of B and part of A on the downthrown side at the surface).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always construct a cross-section **perpendicular to the fault strike** to avoid complications from apparent dip. Use standard map symbols: $U$ for upthrown side, $D$ for downthrown side, with tick marks on the downthrown side.
