## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Define the term "drag fold" and explain how it helps in determining the sense of movement along a fault.**

**Model Answer:**

A **drag fold** is a small-scale fold formed in the strata immediately adjacent to a fault plane, caused by the frictional resistance offered by the fault surface during relative movement of the two blocks. The beds are bent or curved toward the fault plane in the direction of the relative movement of the opposite block.

**Significance in determining fault movement:**
- The drag folds always **converge toward the fault** in the direction of relative slip.
- For a **normal fault**: drag folds on the downthrown side bend **toward the fault** in the down-dip direction, indicating that the hanging wall has moved **down and outward**.
- For a **reverse fault**: drag folds on the upthrown side bend toward the fault, indicating that the hanging wall has moved **up and over** the footwall.
- The **asymmetry of the drag** provides a reliable kinematic indicator even when slickensides are absent or weathered.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often describe drag folds as "always curving upward." This is incorrect. The curvature direction depends on the **relative movement sense** of the two blocks, not on gravity. Always analyze the geometry of both sides of the fault.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Distinguish between an outlier and an inlier with suitable sketches.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Outlier | Inlier |
|---------|---------|--------|
| Definition | An isolated patch of **younger** rock completely surrounded by **older** rocks | An isolated patch of **older** rock completely surrounded by **younger** rocks |
| Formation | Resistant cap rock on downthrown block of a normal fault is preserved as an erosional remnant while surrounding softer rock is removed | Erosion breaches a younger cap rock on the upthrown block, exposing older rocks in a window |
| Tectonic Setting | Commonly associated with normal faulting in horizontal or gently dipping strata | Commonly associated with anticlinal structures or upthrown blocks of reverse faults |
| Example Setting | A mesa-like hill preserved within a valley of older rock | A valley eroded into a plateau of younger rock |

**Sketch Description (for the answer script):**

- **Outlier**: Draw a flat-lying sequence of beds A, B, C (A oldest, C youngest) cut by a normal fault. On the downthrown side, erosion has removed part of beds A and B, leaving a remnant of C surrounded by A.
- **Inlier**: Draw the same fault. On the upthrown side, erosion has cut through bed C, exposing bed A as a window within a landscape dominated by C.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Some students write that "outliers are formed by reverse faults." This is **incorrect** in the classical sense. Outliers are predominantly associated with **normal faulting and erosion** in horizontal or gently dipping strata. The term refers to the **erosional remnant**, not the fault type directly.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Describe in detail the various effects of faulting on the outcrops of sedimentary rocks. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The effects of faulting on sedimentary rock outcrops can be grouped into the following categories:

**1. Displacement (Offset) of Beds:**
When a fault cuts through layered sedimentary rocks, the originally continuous beds become physically separated. The amount of offset on a geological map is the **map separation**, which is the apparent horizontal displacement of the beds as seen in plan view. This offset is a function of the throw, heave, slip, fault dip, bed dip, and the angle between the fault strike and bed strike.

**2. Repetition of Strata:**
Repetition occurs when the same stratigraphic layer appears more than once in a sequence as a result of faulting. This is most common in **reverse faults** where beds dip toward the fault and the upthrown block moves over the downthrown block, effectively duplicating part of the section. On a geological map, repeated beds are identified by the **same lithology appearing in two separate outcrop belts** separated by the fault trace.

**3. Omission of Strata:**
Omission is the opposite of repetition, where a layer is missing from the stratigraphic sequence due to faulting. This is most common in **normal faults** where the downthrown block drops, juxtaposing a younger bed on the downthrown side against an older bed on the upthrown side. On a map, the outcrop width of affected beds **decreases or disappears entirely**.

**4. Drag Folding:**
Beds adjacent to the fault plane are bent due to frictional drag. The drag is a reliable kinematic indicator showing the sense of movement of each block.

**5. Slickensides and Fault Breccia:**
The fault plane is often marked by **slickensides** (polished, striated surfaces) and **fault breccia** (crushed, angular rock fragments). These features indicate the **direction of movement** and the **intensity of deformation**.

**6. Geomorphic Effects:**
- **Fault scarps** form along the fault trace.
- **Offset drainage** occurs when streams are displaced by strike-slip faulting.
- **Triangular facets** form along normal fault-bounded mountain fronts.
- **Sag ponds** form in extensional depressions.

**7. Outliers and Inliers:**
As described in Part A, faulting combined with erosion creates outliers (younger rock remnants) and inliers (older rock windows).

**8. Effect on Outcrop Width:**
The width of an outcrop on a map changes across a fault because the beds are displaced vertically. The outcrop width $W = t / \tan\delta$ on flat topography will appear different on the upthrown versus downthrown block due to the additional elevation difference created by the throw.

---

**(b) A normal fault with a dip of 60° has a slip of 80 m. Calculate the throw, heave, and the apparent slip on a vertical cross-section perpendicular to the fault strike. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Fault dip $\phi = 60°$
- Total slip $S = 80$ m
- Cross-section is perpendicular to fault strike (so no apparent dip complications)

**Step 1: Calculate the throw (vertical component of slip):**

$$T = S \cdot \sin\phi$$

$$T = 80 \cdot \sin 60°$$

$$T = 80 \times 0.8660 = 69.28 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the heave (horizontal component of slip):**

$$H = S \cdot \cos\phi$$

$$H = 80 \cdot \cos 60°$$

$$H = 80 \times 0.5000 = 40.00 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the apparent slip on the cross-section:**

Since the cross-section is perpendicular to the fault strike, the apparent slip **equals the true slip** because there is no horizontal component parallel to the section that needs to be projected.

$$\text{Apparent Slip} = S = 80 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Verify using the Pythagorean relationship:**

$$S = \sqrt{T^2 + H^2} = \sqrt{(69.28)^2 + (40.00)^2}$$

$$S = \sqrt{4800 + 1600} = \sqrt{6400} = 80 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Throw | 69.28 m |
| Heave | 40.00 m |
| Total Slip | 80.00 m |
| Apparent Slip (in dip section) | 80.00 m |

**Geological Interpretation:**
- The throw of 69.28 m indicates significant vertical displacement, suggesting an active or recently active normal fault.
- The heave of 40 m means the downthrown block has moved 40 m horizontally away from the fault.
- In the field, this would produce a **prominent fault scarp** of approximately 69 m height (before erosion).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the formation of outliers and inliers with reference to faulting and erosion. How do they differ from each other? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Outliers — Formation:**

Outliers are isolated patches of younger rock completely surrounded by older rocks. They form through the combined action of **faulting and differential erosion**:

1. A **normal fault** displaces a sequence of horizontal or gently dipping sedimentary beds.
2. The **downthrown block** carries a resistant cap rock (e.g., sandstone) downward.
3. **Erosion** preferentially removes the softer, surrounding layers (e.g., shale) on the downthrown side.
4. The **resistant cap rock** is preserved as a small, mesa-like hill or hill range.
5. This remnant of younger rock (the cap) is now surrounded by **older rocks** (the underlying softer layers) that have been exhumed by erosion.

**Example:** An outlier of Deccan Trap basalt (younger) sitting within a landscape of older granite.

**Inliers — Formation:**

Inliers are isolated patches of older rock completely surrounded by younger rocks. They form by:

1. A **normal fault** or **anticlinal structure** brings older rocks closer to the surface on the upthrown block.
2. **Erosion** cuts through the younger cap rock on the upthrown block, breaching it completely.
3. A "**window**" is opened in the cap rock, exposing the older rocks beneath.
4. The exposed older rock patch is entirely surrounded by the **younger cap rock**.

**Example:** An inlier of Precambrian basement gneiss (older) exposed through a window in Phanerozoic sediment cover (younger).

**Differences Table:**

| Feature | Outlier | Inlier |
|---------|---------|--------|
| Surrounding Rock | Older | Younger |
| Central Rock | Younger | Older |
| Position Relative to Fault | Downthrown block (resistant cap preserved) | Upthrown block (cap breached by erosion) |
| Erosional Process | Removal of softer surrounding layers | Removal of resistant cap rock in a localized area |
| Topographic Expression | Hill or mesa | Valley or depression |
| Tectonic Association | Normal faulting, monoclinal flexures | Anticlinal crests, upthrown blocks |

**Field Identification:**
- **Outliers** appear as **positive topographic features** (hills) on a map, with the youngest rocks at the center.
- **Inliers** appear as **negative topographic features** (valleys) on a map, with the oldest rocks at the center.

---

**(b) Three sedimentary beds A, B, and C (A oldest, C youngest) strike N-S and dip 30°E. A normal fault strikes E-W and dips 60°S with a throw of 50 m. Construct a labeled cross-section perpendicular to the fault and determine the effect of faulting on the outcrop pattern. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Establish the geometric framework.**

- Beds: A (bottom, oldest), B (middle), C (top, youngest)
- Bed strike: N-S
- Bed dip: 30°E
- Fault strike: E-W
- Fault dip: 60°S
- Fault type: Normal
- Throw: 50 m

**Step 2: Determine which block is downthrown.**

The fault dips 60°S, meaning it goes **downward to the south**. Therefore, the **southern block is the downthrown block** (hanging wall), and the **northern block is the upthrown block** (footwall).

**Step 3: Calculate the heave of the fault.**

$$H = \frac{T}{\tan\phi} = \frac{50}{\tan 60°} = \frac{50}{1.732} = 28.87 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Analyze the outcrop pattern.**

The cross-section is taken along an **E-W line** (perpendicular to both the fault strike and the bed strike). On this section:

**Upthrown (North) Block:**
- Beds A, B, C appear in normal stratigraphic order.
- The bed A is at the bottom, C at the top, all dipping 30°E.

**Downthrown (South) Block:**
- Beds A, B, C appear in normal order, but the entire sequence is **vertically offset by 50 m downward**.
- The cross-section shows that **bed A on the downthrown side is now at the same topographic level as bed C on the upthrown side**, assuming the throw equals the stratigraphic separation.

**Step 5: Identify the effect — Omission of Strata.**

At the fault contact on the cross-section:
- Upthrown side exposes bed C (youngest) at the surface near the fault.
- Downthrown side exposes bed A (oldest) at the surface near the fault.

This means that **strata B and parts of A and C are "omitted"** from the surface sequence at the fault contact. The outcrop pattern shows **older rocks on the downthrown block** juxtaposed against **younger rocks on the upthrown block** — a classic signature of a **normal fault with omission**.

**Step 6: Cross-Section Diagram (Text Description for Answer Script):**

```
                CROSS-SECTION (E-W direction)
    
    North (Upthrown)                          South (Downthrown)
    
    C \\\\\\\\\\\\\\                          C \\\\\\\\\\\\\\
    B \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\                         B \\\\\\\\\\\\\
    A \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\                        A \\\\\\\\\\\\
    \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\                        \\\\\\\\\\\\
    ===================FAULT 60S==============  
    (50 m throw)
    Bed C (U) | Bed A (D)
    
    Legend: \ = bed; U = upthrown; D = downthrown
```

**Step 7: Geological Interpretation.**

- The throw of 50 m has **juxtaposed older rocks (A) on the south against younger rocks (C) on the north**.
- This is a **stratigraphic separation of approximately 50 m vertical, or 28.87 m horizontal** along the cross-section.
- The **outcrop width** of each bed will be **greater on the downthrown block** because the same vertical thickness is now spread over a longer horizontal distance due to the additional elevation difference.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When constructing cross-sections, students commonly:
> 1. **Draw the fault plane in the wrong dip direction** — always verify with the map data.
> 2. **Forget to mark the downthrown side with tick marks** (standard KTU convention).
> 3. **Misidentify repetition vs. omission** — use the rule: in normal faults, **strata are omitted** on the downthrown side where younger beds meet older beds across the fault.
> 4. **Calculate heave incorrectly** — remember: $H = T / \tan(\phi)$, not $H = T \times \tan(\phi)$.
> 5. **Ignore the bed dip direction** — if the bed dips 30°E and the fault dips 60°S, the section is taken in the **E-W direction** (perpendicular to both strikes).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Fault scarp** = cliff formed directly by faulting (primary) or by differential erosion along an old fault (fault-line scarp).
- [ ] **Drag fold** = bent strata adjacent to fault; curvature indicates slip direction.
- [ ] **Outlier** = younger rock surrounded by older rock; formed by **resistant cap preserved on downthrown block + erosion**.
- [ ] **Inlier** = older rock surrounded by younger rock; formed by **erosion breaching cap on upthrown block**.
- [ ] **Repetition** = same bed appears twice; common in **reverse faults** with beds dipping toward fault.
- [ ] **Omission** = bed is missing from sequence; common in **normal faults** with beds dipping toward fault.
- [ ] **Slickensides** = polished, striated fault surface; indicates slip direction.
- [ ] **Fault breccia** = crushed rock fragments in fault zone; indicates fault intensity.
- [ ] **Offset drainage** = streams displaced by strike-slip faults; key aerial photo indicator.
- [ ] **Triangular facets** = triangular erosional remnants along normal fault-bounded ranges; indicate active extension.
- [ ] **Apparent dip formula**: $\tan\delta_a = \tan\delta \cdot \cos\theta$
- [ ] **True dip from apparent dip**: $\tan\delta = \tan\delta_a / \cos\theta$
- [ ] **Slip = throw × cosec(fault dip) = heave × sec(fault dip)**
- [ ] **Outcrop width on flat ground**: $W = t / \tan\delta$ — decreases as dip increases.
- [ ] **Outcrop width on slope**: $W = t / \sin(\delta \pm \alpha)$
- [ ] **Rule of V's in valleys**: outcrops form a V that points **upstream in dipping beds** and is **symmetric in horizontal beds**.
- [ ] **Always construct cross-sections perpendicular to the fault strike** to avoid apparent dip issues.
- [ ] **KTU convention**: tick marks on the downthrown side of normal faults; $U$ and $D$ labels on both sides.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for repetition/omission**: "**NO**rmal = **O**mission" (when beds dip toward the downthrown block).
- [ ] **For horizontal beds + normal fault**: downthrown block may host **outliers**; upthrown block may host **inliers** after erosion.

**Top 5 Common Mistakes to Avoid in KTU Exams:**

1. Confusing **slip** with **separation** (slip is true movement; separation is map-measured).
2. Using **wrong sign convention** for apparent dip (true dip is always ≥ apparent dip).
3. Drawing **faults in the wrong dip direction** on cross-sections.
4. Forgetting to **convert units** (degrees to radians, meters to km when needed).
5. Misidentifying **outliers as inliers** or vice versa (always check whether central rock is older or younger).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geologic/Geomorphic Criteria for Recognition of Faults and Fault Plane Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET615 - STRUCTURAL GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Geologic/Geomorphic Criteria for Recognition of Faults and Fault Plane Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault:** A **fault** is a planar fracture or discontinuity in a volume of rock across which there has been measurable displacement of the rock mass parallel to the fracture surface (as defined in standard structural geology references and aligned with the KTU OECET615 syllabus).

**Geologic Criteria for Recognition of Faults** are direct, rock-based field evidences (e.g., offset strata, fault breccia, slickensides, mylonite, drag folds) that confirm a fault's existence regardless of surface topography.

**Geomorphic Criteria** are landscape-level features (e.g., fault scarps, triangular facets, offset drainage, sag ponds, linear valleys) that betray the presence of a fault through surface morphology and erosional/depositional patterns.

**Fault Plane Solution (FPS)** — also called a **focal mechanism solution** or **beach-ball diagram** — is a graphical representation (typically on a lower-hemisphere equal-area stereographic projection) of the orientation of a fault plane and the slip direction derived from the polarity of first P-wave arrivals recorded by seismographs surrounding the earthquake source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** A *Fault Plane Solution* does NOT directly image the fault. Instead, it interprets the radiating seismic waves to infer the **orientation of the fault**, the **direction of slip**, and the **regional stress axes** (P, B, T).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Real-World Analogy (The Cracked Biscuit / The Broken Road):**
> Imagine snapping a chocolate bar diagonally and then sliding the two halves past each other:
> - The **chocolate layers no longer line up** → equivalent to *offset beds*.
> - **Tiny crumbs and powder** fall along the break → equivalent to *fault breccia / gouge*.
> - The **scratch marks on the sliding surface** → equivalent to *slickensides*.
> - The **step-like edge** you see on the road after an earthquake → equivalent to a *fault scarp*.
> - The **direction you pushed the halves** → equivalent to the **slip vector** on a *fault plane solution*.

For the **focal mechanism**, think of an *inflating balloon* inside the Earth: the way the balloon pushes outward creates compressional P-waves in some directions and tensional (dilatational) pull in others. A seismologist listens to the pattern of "pushes" vs "pulls" at many stations and reconstructs the geometry of the fault rupture — much like reconstructing the orientation of a coin by listening to how sound bounces off it.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| P-wave velocity (upper crust) | $V_p$ | **5.5 – 6.5 km/s** |
| S-wave velocity (upper crust) | $V_s$ | **3.2 – 3.8 km/s** |
| Poisson's ratio | $\nu$ | **0.25** (typical crustal) |
| Shear modulus (crustal avg.) | $\mu$ | **~3 × 10¹⁰ Pa** |
| Dip of fault plane | $\delta$ | **0°–90°** |
| Rake (pitch) of slip | $\lambda$ | **−180° to +180°** |
| Strike of fault | $\phi$ | **0°–360°** (azimuth) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Geologic Criteria for Recognition of Faults

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Geologic Criteria Matter:** They are diagnostic, three-dimensional, and remain valid even when surface expression is eroded or buried.

1. **Offset / Displaced Beds, Veins, or Dikes**
   - A bed, vein, dike, or sedimentary layer with a known continuity is cut and shifted across a plane. This is the most diagnostic criterion.
   - Apparent offset is **horizontal** (strike-slip), **vertical** (dip-slip), or **oblique**.

2. **Repetition and Omission of Strata**
   - **Reverse / Thrust fault:** repetition of beds in a section.
   - **Normal fault:** omission of beds in a section.
   - Determined by stratigraphic column matching.

3. **Fault Breccia and Cataclasite**
   - **Fault breccia:** angular fragments (>2 mm) cemented in a fine matrix.
   - **Cataclasite:** rock with fragmented fabric formed by brittle frictional sliding.
   - Indicates brittle deformation along a fault zone.

4. **Fault Gouge**
   - Uncemented, clay-rich powdery material formed by pulverization in the fault core.

5. **Mylonite**
   - Fine-grained, foliated, ductilely-deformed rock produced by crystal-plastic flow in deeper crustal fault zones (ductile shear zones).

6. **Slickensides and Slickenlines (Slickenfibres)**
   - **Slickensides:** polished, striated fault surface.
   - **Slickenlines:** the actual grooves.
   - **Slickenfibres:** mineral fibres (e.g., quartz, calcite) grown in the slip direction — these indicate the **sense of slip**.

7. **Drag Folds**
   - Beds are bent ("dragged") near the fault plane due to frictional resistance during movement. The drag sense directly indicates the slip direction.

8. **Silicification, Mineralization, and Alteration Halos**
   - Hydrothermal fluids percolate along faults, depositing quartz, calcite, ore minerals, or producing clay alteration. These linear alteration zones betray fault traces.

9. **Sudden Truncation of Structures**
   - Folds, joints, foliations, or other faults ending abruptly against a planar surface.

10. **Shatter Cones & Pseudotachylite**
    - Diagnostic of **impact faults** and **seismic slip** respectively.

### 2.2 Geomorphic Criteria for Recognition of Faults

> [!NOTE]
> Geomorphic criteria are surface expressions and are particularly valuable in **neotectonic** and **active fault** studies.

1. **Fault Scarp**
   - A linear, steep slope on the Earth's surface produced by differential displacement across a fault.
   - **Free face** (young, unweathered) vs **degraded scarp** (old, eroded).

2. **Triangular Facets**
   - Trapezoidal/triangular spurs along mountain fronts produced by differential uplift and erosion of a fault scarp.

3. **Offset / Deflected Drainage**
   - Streams, river terraces, or alluvial fans systematically displaced across a fault — diagnostic of **strike-slip** motion.

4. **Sag Ponds and Ponded Basins**
   - Small depressions along a fault trace where water accumulates (common along the San Andreas Fault).

5. **Linear Valleys and Troughs**
   - Fault-controlled linear topographic lows formed by preferential erosion of fractured rock.

6. **Pressure Ridges, Shutter Ridges, and Riedel Shears**
   - Compressional uplifts or lateral push-ups along strike-slip faults.

7. **Hot Springs and Spring Lines**
   - Groundwater emerges along permeable fault zones, producing aligned spring lines.

8. **Knickpoints and Waterfalls in Streams**
   - Sudden changes in stream gradient across a fault trace.

9. **Scarp Morphometry (Age Estimation)**
   - **Scarp degradation modelling** using the diffusion equation:
     $$ u(z,t) = u_0 \cdot \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{\kappa t}}\right) $$
   - where $u$ is the topographic offset, $\kappa$ is the **scarp-diffusion coefficient** (typically **0.5–5 m²/kyr**), $t$ is time, and $z$ is height above base. Older scarps have gentler, more convex-up profiles.

### 2.3 Fault Plane Solution (FPS) — Theory

A focal mechanism represents the **double-couple point source** model of an earthquake. The radiation pattern of P-waves has four quadrants:

- **Quadrants of compression (shaded black on beach-ball):** First P-wave motion is **away** from the source (the ground pushes up first).
- **Quadrants of dilatation (white):** First P-wave motion is **toward** the source (the ground pulls down first).

Two perpendicular nodal planes separate these quadrants. **One of them is the fault plane; the other is the auxiliary plane.** Both are kinematically valid — discrimination requires geological evidence.

#### 2.3.1 Stress Axes on the FPS

- **P-axis (Pressure axis):** bisector of the dilatation quadrants; axis of **maximum compression** $\sigma_1$.
- **T-axis (Tension axis):** bisector of the compression quadrants; axis of **minimum compression** $\sigma_3$.
- **B-axis (Null axis):** intersection of the two nodal planes; $\sigma_2$ (intermediate).

#### 2.3.2 Types of Fault Plane Solutions

| Mechanism | Beach-Ball Pattern | Stress Regime |
|---|---|---|
| **Strike-slip** | Four-lobed "bow-tie" on map view | $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ horizontal, $\sigma_2$ vertical |
| **Normal** | White center with two black lobes on sides | $\sigma_1$ vertical, $\sigma_3$ horizontal (extension) |
| **Reverse / Thrust** | Black center with two white lobes on sides | $\sigma_1$ horizontal, $\sigma_3$ vertical (compression) |
| **Oblique** | Asymmetric pattern | Combination |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these for Module 4 derivations and numerical questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\sigma} \cdot \hat{n} - (\vec{\sigma} \cdot \hat{n} \cdot \hat{n})\hat{n}$ | Traction on fault plane with normal $\hat{n}$ |
| 2 | $\tau = \frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)}{2}\sin(2\theta)$ | Shear stress on plane at angle $\theta$ to $\sigma_1$ |
| 3 | $\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\cos(2\theta)$ | Normal stress on plane |
| 4 | $\tan(2\theta_r) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Principal stress orientation |
| 5 | $\phi = \frac{\pi}{2} - \delta$ | Relationship between dip $\delta$ and plunge |
| 6 | $V_p / V_s = \sqrt{\frac{2(1-\nu)}{1-2\nu}}$ | P/S velocity ratio in Poisson solid |
| 7 | $M_0 = \mu A D$ | Seismic moment: $\mu$ = shear modulus, $A$ = rupture area, $D$ = avg. slip |
| 8 | $M_w = \frac{2}{3}\log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07$ | Moment magnitude (Hanks–Kanamori) |
| 9 | $\mathrm{erfc}(x) = 1 - \mathrm{erf}(x)$ | Complementary error function for scarp diffusion |
| 10 | $\lambda = \arctan\!\left(\frac{D_h}{D_v}\right)$ | Rake of slip vector (horizontal $D_h$, vertical $D_v$) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Geological Utility

- **Seismic Hazard Analysis:** FPS databases (e.g., Global CMT) identify active fault orientations and stress regimes — critical for earthquake-resistant **building code design** in Kerala (KSERC, BIS 1893).
- **Tunnel & Dam Site Selection:** Faults are zones of weakness and permeability; geomorphic criteria (e.g., Idukki, Mullaperiyar dam regions) help avoid them.
- **Hydrocarbon & Geothermal Exploration:** Faults act as conduits or seals for fluids; recognition guides drilling.
- **Landslide Risk Mapping:** Fault-controlled fracture systems predispose slopes to failure (Western Ghats, Wayanad, Idukki).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Stress Components on a Fault Plane

**Given:** A 2-D stress tensor in a fault-perpendicular coordinate frame:
$$ \sigma_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_1 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_3 \end{pmatrix} $$
where $\sigma_1 > \sigma_3$, acting on a plane whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with $\sigma_1$.

**Step 1 — Define the unit normal to the plane.**
The plane's normal vector is rotated $\theta$ from the $\sigma_1$ axis:
$$ \hat{n} = (\cos\theta,\ \sin\theta) $$

**Step 2 — Compute traction vector.**
The traction $\vec{T}$ acting on the plane is given by Cauchy's stress theorem:
$$ \vec{T} = \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \hat{n} $$

Substituting:
$$ \vec{T} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_1 & 0 \\ 0 & \sigma_3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_1 \cos\theta \\ \sigma_3 \sin\theta \end{pmatrix} $$

**Step 3 — Resolve traction into normal and shear components.**
The **normal stress** is the component of $\vec{T}$ along $\hat{n}$:
$$ \sigma_n = \vec{T} \cdot \hat{n} = \sigma_1 \cos^2\theta + \sigma_3 \sin^2\theta $$

**Step 4 — Simplify using trigonometric identities.**
Using $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$ and $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$:
$$ \sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\cos(2\theta) $$

**Step 5 — Derive shear stress on the plane.**
The shear traction magnitude is:
$$ \tau = \sqrt{\vert\vec{T}\vert^2 - \sigma_n^2} $$

Alternatively, by the projection perpendicular to $\hat{n}$, the shear vector lies in the plane and has magnitude:
$$ \tau = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_3)\sin\theta\cos\theta $$

**Step 6 — Apply double-angle identity.**
Using $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{1}{2}\sin(2\theta)$:
$$ \tau = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\sin(2\theta) $$

**Final Master Equations** (placed in clean display math):
$$ \sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\cos(2\theta) $$

$$ \tau = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\sin(2\theta) $$

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **Mohr-Coulomb foundation equations** for fault mechanics and govern slip on any pre-existing plane.

### 3.2 Derivation: Orientation of Principal Stresses in 2-D

**Given:** General 2-D stress state:
$$ \sigma_{ij} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y \end{pmatrix} $$

**Step 1 — Eigenvalue problem.**
Principal stresses $\sigma_{1,3}$ are eigenvalues of $\sigma_{ij}$:
$$ \det(\sigma_{ij} - \sigma I) = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Expand determinant.**
$$ (\sigma_x - \sigma)(\sigma_y - \sigma) - \tau_{xy}^2 = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Solve the quadratic.**
$$ \sigma^2 - (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\sigma + (\sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2) = 0 $$

Using the quadratic formula:
$$ \sigma_{1,3} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$

**Step 4 — Find the orientation $\theta_r$ of the principal axes.**
For zero shear on the rotated plane, the equilibrium condition yields:
$$ \tan(2\theta_r) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} $$

**Step 5 — Verify that $\theta_r$ and $\theta_r + 90°$ correspond to $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$.**
The sign of $\sigma_x - \sigma_y$ decides which is which. By convention, $\sigma_1$ aligns with the direction having the larger normal stress.

### 3.3 Derivation: Rake of Slip Vector

**Given:** Slip vector $\vec{s}$ has horizontal component $D_h$ and vertical component $D_v$.

**Step 1 — Define rake (also called pitch or slip angle).**
The rake $\lambda$ is the angle of $\vec{s}$ measured within the fault plane from the strike reference direction (horizontal) to the slip vector.

**Step 2 — Trigonometric relation.**
The slip vector projected into the fault plane has horizontal projection $D_h$ and vertical projection $D_v$, both measured along the fault plane. By elementary trigonometry:
$$ \tan(\lambda) = \frac{D_v}{D_h} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\lambda$.**
$$ \lambda = \arctan\!\left(\frac{D_v}{D_h}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Sign convention (Aki & Richards).**
- $\lambda = 0°$: **Left-lateral strike-slip**
- $\lambda = 180°$: **Right-lateral strike-slip**
- $\lambda = 90°$: **Reverse slip** (hanging wall up)
- $\lambda = -90°$: **Normal slip** (hanging wall down)
- $-90° < \lambda < 90°$: oblique combinations

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A fault plane strikes N45°E, dips 60° SE. The slip vector has $D_h = 3$ m (right-lateral) and $D_v = 1.5$ m (hanging wall up). Find the rake.

**Step 1 — Identify the slip quadrant.**
Right-lateral $+$ hanging-wall up → reverse-dextral oblique regime. Expected $\lambda$ between 0° and 90°.

**Step 2 — Apply rake formula.**
$$ \lambda = \arctan\!\left(\frac{D_v}{D_h}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1.5}{3.0}\right) $$

$$ \lambda = \arctan(0.5) = 26.57° $$

**Step 3 — Interpret the mechanism.**
Since $\lambda = 26.57°$ (between 0° and 90°), the fault is **oblique-slip, reverse-dextral**. On a focal mechanism beach ball, this corresponds to a solution with mixed thrust and strike-slip character.

**Step 4 — Dip verification.**
The dip of 60° SE is consistent with a steeply-dipping oblique fault typical of transpressional tectonic regimes (e.g., parts of the **Alpine-Himalayan belt** or the **Wayanad shear zone** of Kerala).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Criteria ↔ Feature ↔ Diagnostic Power

| Criterion | Type | Diagnostic Power | Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Offset beds | Geologic | **Very High** | Need piercing points |
| Slickenlines | Geologic | **Very High** | Slip sense vs slip timing |
| Drag folds | Geologic | **High** | Drag can also be gravity-driven |
| Fault gouge | Geologic | Moderate | Forms in any brittle fault |
| Mylonite | Geologic | **Very High** | Ductile, deep crustal |
| Fault scarp | Geomorphic | **Very High (active)** | Can be eroded or buried |
| Offset stream | Geomorphic | **Very High** | Needs multiple streams |
| Sag pond | Geomorphic | Moderate | Could be karstic |
| Triangular facet | Geomorphic | Moderate | Glacial valleys mimic |
| Lineament (RS) | Geomorphic | Low–Moderate | Need ground truth |
| Focal mechanism FPS | Seismic | **Very High** | Ambiguity between fault/auxiliary plane |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Fault Recognition Criteria

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fault Recognition Criteria] --> B[Geologic Criteria]
    A --> C[Geomorphic Criteria]
    A --> D[Seismological Criteria]

    B --> B1[Offset Beds and Veins]
    B --> B2[Repetition and Omission of Strata]
    B --> B3[Fault Breccia and Cataclasite]
    B --> B4[Fault Gouge and Mylonite]
    B --> B5[Slickensides and Slickenlines]
    B --> B6[Drag Folds]
    B --> B7[Hydrothermal Alteration]

    C --> C1[Fault Scarps]
    C --> C2[Triangular Facets]
    C --> C3[Offset Drainage]
    C --> C4[Sag Ponds]
    C --> C5[Linear Valleys]
    C --> C6[Knickpoints]

    D --> D1[P wave First Motions]
    D --> D2[Focal Mechanism Beach Ball]
    D --> D3[Stress Axis P B T]
    D --> D4[Nodal Plane Identification]

    B1 --> R1[Highest Diagnostic Value]
    B5 --> R1
    D2 --> R1
    C1 --> R1
    C3 --> R1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fault Plane Solution Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EQ[Earthquake Event] --> S1[Seismic Wave Generation]
    S1 --> S2[Seismograph Network Recording]
    S2 --> S3[P wave First Motion Polarity]
    S3 --> S4[Plot on Stereographic Projection]
    S4 --> S5[Identify Quadrants of Compression and Dilatation]
    S5 --> S6[Draw Nodal Planes]
    S6 --> S7[Determine P T B Axes]
    S7 --> S8[Beach Ball Diagram]
    S8 --> S9[Discriminate Fault Plane vs Auxiliary Plane]
    S9 --> S10[Stress Regime Classification]

    S8 --> A1[Strike Slip]
    S8 --> A2[Normal]
    S8 --> A3[Reverse]
    S8 --> A4[Oblique]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Stress Axes on a Beach Ball

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BB[Beach Ball Focal Mechanism] --> T1[T axis: Minimum Compression]
    BB --> P1[P axis: Maximum Compression]
    BB --> B1[B axis: Intermediate Null]

    P1 --> R1[Inside White Quadrants Bisector]
    T1 --> R2[Inside Black Quadrants Bisector]
    B1 --> R3[Intersection of Two Nodal Planes]

    R1 --> M1[Mechanism Type]
    R2 --> M1
    R3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2A[Strike Slip]
    M1 --> M2B[Normal]
    M1 --> M2C[Reverse]
    M1 --> M2D[Oblique]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Block Model: Geologic Evidence ↔ Geomorphic Surface ↔ Tectonic Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TP[Tectonic Process] --> FR[Fault Rupture]
    FR --> G1[Geologic Signature]
    FR --> G2[Geomorphic Signature]
    FR --> G3[Seismic Signature]

    G1 --> E1[Brittle: Breccia, Gouge]
    G1 --> E2[Ductile: Mylonite]
    G1 --> E3[Slip Indicators: Slickenlines, Drag]
    G1 --> E4[Stratigraphic: Repetition or Omission]

    G2 --> F1[Scarp and Facet]
    G2 --> F2[Stream Offset]
    G2 --> F3[Sag Pond and Linear Valley]

    G3 --> SE1[Seismic Waves]
    SE1 --> SE2[Beach Ball]
    SE2 --> SE3[Fault Plane Solution]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three geomorphic criteria used for the recognition of faults in the field.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Fault scarp:** A linear, steep topographic break produced by differential displacement across the fault.
> 2. **Offset / deflected drainage:** Rivers, streams, or terraces that are systematically displaced laterally by strike-slip movement.
> 3. **Triangular facets:** Trapezoidal spur faces along a mountain front produced by headward erosion of fault scarps.
> *(Any other valid criteria — sag ponds, linear valleys, spring lines — also accepted. 1 mark each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a fault plane solution. What is the difference between the P-axis and T-axis on a focal mechanism?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - A **fault plane solution (FPS)** is a graphical representation, generally on a lower-hemisphere equal-area stereonet (beach-ball), of the orientation of a fault and the slip direction derived from the polarity of first P-wave arrivals (1 mark).
> - **P-axis:** bisector of the dilatation (white) quadrants, representing the axis of **maximum compressive stress** $\sigma_1$ (1 mark).
> - **T-axis:** bisector of the compressional (shaded) quadrants, representing the axis of **minimum compressive stress** $\sigma_3$ (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module 4 Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **geologic criteria** for the recognition of faults, with neat sketches where appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and interpretation of a **focal mechanism (fault plane solution)**, including the P, B, T axes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

> **Introduction (1 mark):**
> Geologic criteria are direct rock-based field evidences of faulting observed in outcrops, mine walls, drill cores, and tunnels. They are independent of surface weathering and remain diagnostic even where geomorphic features are absent.

> **Main Criteria (5 marks):**
>
> 1. **Offset Beds / Veins / Dikes:** A once-continuous marker is displaced across a planar surface. The sense and amount of separation gives slip direction and magnitude.
> 2. **Repetition & Omission of Strata:** In a normal fault, beds are omitted; in a reverse fault, beds are repeated. Cross-checking stratigraphic columns is diagnostic.
> 3. **Fault Breccia / Cataclasite:** Angular fragments produced by brittle crushing; indicates fault zone.
> 4. **Fault Gouge:** Pulverized clay-rich material in the fault core.
> 5. **Mylonite:** Ductile-shear product, fine-grained, foliated; indicates deep-crustal faulting.
> 6. **Slickensides & Slickenfibres:** Polished, striated surfaces. Slickenfibres (mineral growth in slip direction) give the **sense of slip**.
> 7. **Drag Folds:** Beds bent near the fault plane; geometry indicates the slip direction.
> 8. **Hydrothermal alteration & mineralization:** Linear halos of quartz, calcite, clay, or ore minerals trace fault zones.
> 9. **Sudden truncation of structures:** Folds, foliations, or other faults ending abruptly.
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):**
> A combination of multiple geologic criteria is generally used to confirm fault presence and characterize its kinematics.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

> **Introduction (1 mark):**
> A fault plane solution (FPS), or focal mechanism, is a graphical model of an earthquake source. It is based on the **double-couple point source** assumption, where slip on a fault produces a four-quadrant radiation pattern of P-waves.

> **Construction (3 marks):**
> 1. P-wave first-motion polarities (compressional = "up", dilatational = "down") are plotted on a **lower-hemisphere equal-area stereonet** (Schmidt net) centered on the hypocenter.
> 2. Stations are placed at their azimuth and take-off angle from the source.
> 3. The data are divided into two sets by **two perpendicular great circles** (nodal planes) that separate the two polarities.
> 4. The "beach-ball" is shaded where P-waves are compressional.
> 5. The two nodal planes represent the **fault plane** and the **auxiliary plane** (mathematically equivalent).

> **P, B, T axes (2 marks):**
> - **P-axis:** bisector of the dilatation (white) quadrants; direction of maximum compressive stress $\sigma_1$.
> - **T-axis:** bisector of the compressional (black) quadrants; direction of minimum compressive stress $\sigma_3$.
> - **B-axis:** intersection of the two nodal planes; intermediate stress $\sigma_2$.

> **Interpretation (1 mark):**
> The mechanism (strike-slip, normal, reverse, oblique) and the orientation of the fault constrain the regional tectonic stress field and identify the active structure.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Discuss the **geomorphic criteria** used in recognizing faults, with reference to active fault studies and neotectonic mapping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fault plane strikes N60°E and dips 50° SE. The slip vector has horizontal component 4 m and vertical component 2 m. Determine the **rake** and the **sense of slip**. State the type of focal mechanism expected. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

> **Introduction (1 mark):**
> Geomorphic criteria are surface landform expressions of faulting. They are essential in identifying **active faults** in regions with poor bedrock exposure and form the basis of **neotectonic** and **paleoseismic** studies.

> **Main Criteria (5 marks):**
> 1. **Fault Scarp:** A linear topographic break; the **free face** is young and unweathered, the **degraded scarp** is older and rounded. The profile follows a diffusion equation useful in dating.
> 2. **Triangular Facets:** Trapezoidal faces on fault-controlled mountain fronts.
> 3. **Offset / Deflected Drainage:** Streams displaced laterally by strike-slip motion; multiple offsets of different ages give a **slip-rate** estimate.
> 4. **Sag Ponds:** Closed depressions along fault traces where water accumulates.
> 5. **Linear Valleys & Troughs:** Zones of weak, fractured rock preferentially eroded.
> 6. **Pressure / Shutter Ridges:** Compressional uplifts along strike-slip faults.
> 7. **Spring Lines and Hot Springs:** Aligned groundwater emergence along permeable faults.
> 8. **Knickpoints and Waterfalls:** Abrupt stream-gradient changes across a fault.
> 9. **Scarp Morphometry & Diffusion Dating:** Quantitative age estimates from scarp profiles.

> **Application (1 mark):**
> These criteria underpin paleoseismic trenching, slip-rate estimation, and seismic hazard zoning (e.g., the Himalayan Frontal Thrust, the San Andreas Fault, the **Wayanad and Idukki** regions of Kerala).

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Step 1 — Identify components.**
- Horizontal slip $D_h = 4$ m
- Vertical slip $D_v = 2$ m
- Strike N60°E, Dip 50° SE

**Step 2 — Apply rake formula.**
$$ \lambda = \arctan\!\left(\frac{D_v}{D_h}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2}{4}\right) $$

$$ \lambda = \arctan(0.5) = 26.57° $$

**Step 3 — Determine sense of slip.**
- $D_h = +4$ m (positive horizontal) → **right-lateral** component.
- $D_v = +2$ m (hanging wall up) → **reverse** component.
- Since $0° < \lambda < 90°$, the slip is **reverse-dextral oblique**.

**Step 4 — Expected focal mechanism.**
- The pattern will be a **mixed reverse and strike-slip solution**, with a beach ball showing partly compressional (black) central region and asymmetric lobing typical of **transpression**.

**Step 5 — Final answer.**
$$ \boxed{\lambda = 26.57°,\ \text{Reverse-Dextral Oblique Slip, Transpressional Mechanism}} $$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 4 Fault Recognition Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "slickensides" with "slickenlines"** — slickensides are the polished surface, slickenlines are the striations. (1 mark lost each time confused.)
> 2. **Mixing up repetition/omission** — repetition occurs in **reverse** faults (older over younger), omission in **normal** faults. Reversing these costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing the P-axis with the T-axis** — P is **maximum** compression, T is **minimum**. The bisectors of **white quadrants** give P; the bisectors of **black quadrants** give T.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify a hemisphere** on stereographic projection — always state "lower-hemisphere equal-area projection."
> 5. **Failing to draw a labelled beach ball** in FPS questions — a sketch with P, B, T axes labelled is mandatory for full marks.
> 6. **Sign of rake** — students often omit units or fail to specify the quadrant (e.g., 0°–90° vs −90° to 0°).
> 7. **Geomorphic vs Geologic confusion** — examiners specifically test this distinction; misclassifying drag fold as geomorphic costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — One-Glance Summary**

- A **fault** is a fracture with measurable slip parallel to the surface.
- **Geologic criteria** = rock-based; **Geomorphic criteria** = surface-based; **Seismological criteria** = wave-based.
- **Fault breccia** = angular fragments; **cataclasite** = fragmented fabric; **gouge** = clay-rich powder; **mylonite** = ductile, foliated.
- **Slickensides** = polished surface; **slickenlines** = striations; **slickenfibres** = mineral growths showing slip direction.
- **Drag folds** sense = slip direction.
- **Repetition** = reverse fault; **Omission** = normal fault.
- **Geomorphic markers**: fault scarp, triangular facet, offset stream, sag pond, linear valley, knickpoint, spring line, pressure ridge.
- **Scarp diffusion** equation: $u(z,t) = u_0 \cdot \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{\kappa t}}\right)$.
- **Focal mechanism (FPS)** = beach-ball diagram on lower-hemisphere equal-area stereonet.
- **P-axis** = bisector of white (dilatation) quadrants = $\sigma_1$ (max compression).
- **T-axis** = bisector of black (compressional) quadrants = $\sigma_3$ (min compression).
- **B-axis** = intersection of nodal planes = $\sigma_2$ (intermediate).
- **Two nodal planes** = fault plane + auxiliary plane (both kinematically valid; geological evidence discriminates).
- **Normal FPS** = white center, black side lobes (extension).
- **Reverse FPS** = black center, white side lobes (compression).
- **Strike-slip FPS** = four-lobed "bow-tie" pattern.
- **Rake formula**: $\lambda = \arctan(D_v / D_h)$; quadrant determines sense.
- **Shear & normal stress on a fault plane** (Mohr): $\tau = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\sin(2\theta)$; $\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_3}{2} + \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}\cos(2\theta)$.
- **Seismic moment**: $M_0 = \mu A D$; **Moment magnitude**: $M_w = \frac{2}{3}\log_{10}(M_0) - 6.07$.
- Always specify **lower-hemisphere** projection and include **labelled P, B, T axes** in FPS diagrams.
- For KTU valuation: pair every claim with a **diagrammatic sketch or beach ball** to secure full marks.
- Use the **analytical matrix** (Section 3.5) as a last-minute comparison tool for cross-checking criteria in two-mark sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET615 - STRUCTURAL GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lineaments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Lineaments: Section 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A **lineament** is a mappable, simple or composite linear surface feature (e.g., a straight valley, ridge, escarpment, tonal band, or fracture trace) whose parts lie in straight to gently curving alignment and which differs distinctly from the patterns of surrounding features. Lineaments are widely interpreted as surface expressions of underlying structural elements such as **joints, faults, shear zones, fold axes, lithological contacts, or crustal discontinuities**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textbook Definition Anchor (Un-attributed, Standard)**
> Lineaments are regional-scale linear or arcuate features that extend for several kilometers to hundreds of kilometers on the Earth's surface. They are most reliably identified on **aerial photographs, satellite imagery (Landsat, Sentinel-2, ASTER, LISS-III)**, and digital elevation models (DEMs). Their systematic mapping and analysis constitute an essential component of **structural, tectonic, and engineering geological investigations**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Working Definition |
|---|---|
| **Lineament** | A linear surface feature with tectonic/structural origin. |
| **Photolineament** | A lineament identified solely on aerial photographs or satellite images. |
| **Fracture Trace** | A natural linear feature on the Earth's surface, <1.6 km long, usually discernible on aerial photos. |
| **Line** | A generic term for any line drawn on a map; not necessarily a real surface feature. |
| **Megalineament** | A continent-scale lineament exceeding 100 km in length. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Cracked Eggshell**
> Imagine the Earth's rigid outer crust as the shell of a hard-boiled egg. When the egg is squeezed, the shell cracks along **straight, predictable lines** radiating from the pressure points. Similarly, when tectonic forces squeeze, stretch, or twist a region of the crust, the rock breaks along **preferred directions of weakness** — these surface "cracks" are lineaments. Just as the eggshell cracks reveal the **direction of applied force**, the orientation (azimuth) of lineaments reveals the **regional stress field** (σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ orientations) acting on the bedrock.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters to a Kerala geologist**
> In the Western Ghats, the Laterite uplands, and the fracture-dominated hard-rock aquifers of Kasaragod, Palakkad, and Kollam, lineaments often act as **preferential groundwater pathways** and **landslide-release planes**. Mapping them is a routine pre-requisite for site selection of dams, tunnels, roads, and high-rise foundations.

### 1.4 Physical / Geometric Parameters (Standard Metrics)

The standard geometric attributes recorded for every mapped lineament are:

- **Length (L)** — typically measured in **meters (m)** or **kilometers (km)**. Standard cut-offs: **≥ 1 km** for regional mapping.
- **Azimuth (θ)** — the compass bearing of the line, measured **clockwise from true north (0°–360°)**. Standard class-interval for rose diagrams: **10°**.
- **Orientation / Strike** — equivalent to azimuth; expressed in **degrees** or quadrant notation (e.g., N 35° E).
- **Spatial Density (D)** — total lineament length per unit area, expressed in **km/km²** or **m/m²** (e.g., **2.5 km/km²** for moderately fractured terrain).
- **Frequency (F)** — number of lineaments per unit area, in **count/km²** (e.g., **0.8 km⁻²**).
- **Cross-cutting relationships** — a relative-age criterion: the lineament that displaces the other is **younger**.

### 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Linkage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Alignment**
> - **CO3 (Module 4)**: *Identify and analyse lineaments from maps and remote-sensing data, and interpret their tectonic significance.*
> - This topic directly addresses **Bloom's Level 2 (Understand)** and **Level 3 (Apply)** — students must *define*, *identify*, *measure*, and *interpret* lineaments from given image/map data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4 — Lineaments: Section 2

## 2.1 Classification of Lineaments (High-Yield for KTU)

Lineaments may be classified on the basis of **origin**, **morphology**, or **scale**.

### 2.1.1 Genetic Classification (Most commonly asked in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Six standard genetic classes of lineaments**

1. **Structural / Tectonic Lineaments** — directly express joints, faults, shear zones, axial traces of folds, or thrust planes. *Example: Bhavani lineament, Tamil Nadu.*
2. **Lithological / Bedrock Lineaments** — reflect differential erosion of dipping strata, dykes, sills, or contrasting rock units. *Example: Quartz-reef ridges in Aravalli terrain.*
3. **Geomorphological Lineaments** — surface landforms such as aligned river valleys, escarpments, and drainage anomalies that follow underlying structural grain. *Example: The aligned west-flowing rivers of Kerala following the NNW-SSE structural trend.*
4. **Tonal / Spectral Lineaments** — visible only on multi-spectral or thermal imagery as straight colour/tone differences, often related to soil moisture, vegetation stress, or rock-type contrasts. *Example: NDVI anomalies along fracture zones.*
5. **Volcanic / Igneous Lineaments** — surface traces of dyke swarms, volcanic rifts, or ring dykes. *Example: Newer Dolerite dyke swarms of the Deccan.*
6. **Anthropogenic / Cultural Lineaments** — straight roads, field boundaries, canals mimicking structural trends. **Must be filtered out** during structural mapping.

### 2.1.2 Morphological Classification

| Class | Curvature | Typical Length |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight lineaments** | Negligible curvature | 1 – 100 km |
| **Slightly curved** | Gentle arc | 1 – 50 km |
| **Arcuate / Curved** | Pronounced curve, often fault-bend folds | 5 – 200 km |
| **Megalineaments** | Mostly straight, regional scale | > 100 km |

### 2.1.3 Scale Classification

| Scale | Length Range | Method of Identification |
|---|---|---|
| **Local** | < 1 km | Field mapping, drone imagery |
| **Regional** | 1 – 100 km | Aerial photos, satellite images (Landsat, ASTER) |
| **Continental / Megalineament** | > 100 km | Mosaic of satellite scenes, geophysical data |

## 2.2 Methods of Lineament Identification & Extraction (Syllabus-Focused Workflow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Workflow**
> The standard academic procedure for lineament analysis is a **five-stage pipeline** that students must memorize:

**Step 1 — Data Acquisition**
- Acquire satellite imagery (e.g., **Landsat 8 OLI**, **Sentinel-2 MSI**, **ASTER**), aerial photographs, **SRTM / ASTER GDEM** (30 m / 12.5 m), and existing geological maps (GSI Quadrangle sheets on **1:50,000** scale).
- Target resolution: **≤ 30 m** for lineaments **≥ 1 km**.

**Step 2 — Pre-Processing**
- Apply **radiometric calibration, atmospheric correction (FLAASH / DOS)**, pansharpening, and contrast enhancement (linear / histogram equalization).
- Generate hill-shade DEMs from multiple azimuths (commonly **45°, 135°, 225°, 315°**) to highlight subtle linear features.

**Step 3 — Visual Interpretation**
- Use standard **interpretation keys**: tone, texture, pattern, shape, association, shadow.
- **Edge detection** filters (Sobel, Roberts, Canny) and **directional filters** highlight linear features.

**Step 4 — Semi-Automatic / Automatic Extraction**
- Algorithms: **PCI Geomatica LINE module**, **Hough transform**, **Canny edge detection**, **Fractalyse**, **ArcGIS lineament extraction tool**.
- Output: vector polylines with length and azimuth attributes.

**Step 5 — Validation & Tectonic Interpretation**
- Cross-check with **geological maps**, **geophysical data (gravity, magnetic, EM)**, and **field checks**.
- Prepare **rose diagrams**, **length-azimuth tables**, and **lineament density maps** (kernel density in GIS).

## 2.3 Engineering & Tectonic Significance (Why KTU Tests This)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering applications — these are the "Use" questions KTU loves to ask**

- **Engineering Geology** — lineament intersections are **zones of weakness** and are avoided or specially treated in dam, tunnel, and high-rise construction.
- **Hydrogeology** — lineaments in hard-rock terrain (e.g., gneiss, granulite, charnockite) act as **aquifers**; lineament-density maps are used to locate **bore wells** in Kerala's drought-prone laterite zones.
- **Mineral / Hydrocarbon Exploration** — many ore deposits (Au, base metals) and hydrocarbon traps are controlled by **lineament/fault intersections**.
- **Seismic Hazard Assessment** — active lineaments may correspond to **capable faults**; avoiding them is mandatory for **nuclear power plant site selection** (AERB safety code).
- **Landslide & Slope-Stability Studies** — lineaments control the orientation of **joint sets** that govern **planar and wedge failure** in the Western Ghats.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Boundary Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All numerical answers for the KTU ESE numerical on lineament analysis will use the following boxed formulas. Memorize them.**

### 2.4.1 Core Statistical Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Range (India) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lineament Density (D)** | $D = \dfrac{\sum L_{i}}{A}$ | km/km² | 0.5 – 3.0 |
| 2 | **Lineament Frequency (F)** | $F = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | count/km² | 0.2 – 2.0 |
| 3 | **Mean Azimuth (θ̄)** | $\bar{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\sum \sin 2\theta_{i}}{\sum \cos 2\theta_{i}}\right)$ | degrees | 0° – 180° |
| 4 | **Resultant Vector Length (R)** | $R = \sqrt{\left(\sum \sin 2\theta_{i}\right)^{2} + \left(\sum \cos 2\theta_{i}\right)^{2}}$ | dimensionless | 0 – N |
| 5 | **Variance of Azimuth (S²)** | $S^{2} = 1 - \dfrac{R}{N}$ | dimensionless | 0 – 1 (1 = isotropic) |
| 6 | **Mean Length (L̄)** | $\bar{L} = \dfrac{\sum L_{i}}{N}$ | km | 0.5 – 5.0 |
| 7 | **Lineament Intersection Density (I)** | $I = \dfrac{\text{Number of intersections}}{A}$ | count/km² | 0.05 – 0.5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Latex in Tables:** In the table above, every mathematical symbol uses `\vert` / `\mid` style or LaTeX, NOT the vertical pipe character. The pipe character `|` is reserved as the markdown column separator.

### 2.4.2 Notation Used

- $L_{i}$ = length of the *i-th* lineament
- $N$ = total number of lineaments
- $A$ = area of the study region
- $\theta_{i}$ = azimuth of the *i-th* lineament
- $S^{2}$ = circular variance, **S² → 0** means **strong preferred orientation**, **S² → 1** means **isotropic random pattern**.

### 2.4.3 Physical & Standard Constants (Highlight)

- **Earth radius (Rₑ)** = **6371 km**
- **Standard geological map scale (KTU exam questions)** = **1:50,000 (GSI Quadrangle)**
- **Scale of 1° azimuth** on a 1:50,000 map = ~ **0.88 mm** (negligible)
- **Standard rose-diagram class interval** = **10°** (36 petals)
- **Standard rose-diagram class interval (regional)** = **30°** (12 petals)
- **Satellite resolutions commonly used**:
  - **Landsat 8 OLI** = 30 m multispectral, 15 m pan
  - **Sentinel-2** = 10 m, 20 m, 60 m
  - **ASTER VNIR** = 15 m
  - **CARTOSAT-1** = 2.5 m pan
  - **CARTOSAT-2** = 0.65 m pan
- **Field-scale rule (fracture trace vs. lineament)** = length **≥ 1.6 km** → lineament; < 1.6 km → fracture trace (after **Lattman, 1958** standard practice).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4 — Lineaments: Section 3

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivations of Lineament Statistics (ESE-Standard Working)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methodology used below**
> Each formula in the KTU cheat-sheet (Section 2.4) is now derived step-by-step in a way that an examiner can award **full 7/7 marks** for any "calculate lineament density / mean azimuth / variance" sub-part.

---

### 3.1.1 Derivation 1 — Lineament Density (D)

**Statement.** Lineament density is defined as the *total lineament length per unit area*.

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
Let there be $N$ lineaments, indexed $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$, with each lineament having a length $L_{i}$ (in km). Let the total study area be $A$ (in km²).

**Step 2 — Sum the lengths.**
The total length of all lineaments is the arithmetic sum of individual lengths:

$$\sum L_{i} = L_{1} + L_{2} + L_{3} + \dots + L_{N}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by the area.**

$$D = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} L_{i}}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Units check.**
$L_{i}$ is in **km** and $A$ is in **km²**, so the ratio has units of **km/km²** (kilometre of lineament per square kilometre of ground).

**Step 5 — Worked example (KTU-style).**

> A study area of **100 km²** contains 25 lineaments of lengths (in km): 2.1, 3.5, 1.8, 4.2, 2.9, 1.5, 3.0, 2.4, 1.7, 3.8, 2.6, 4.0, 2.2, 3.3, 1.9, 2.7, 3.6, 2.0, 4.1, 2.5, 3.2, 1.6, 2.8, 3.4, 2.3.
>
> Compute $D$.

**Step 5.1 — Sum the lengths.** Group into convenient sets of five to minimize arithmetic error:

- (2.1 + 3.5 + 1.8 + 4.2 + 2.9) = 14.5 km
- (1.5 + 3.0 + 2.4 + 1.7 + 3.8) = 12.4 km
- (2.6 + 4.0 + 2.2 + 3.3 + 1.9) = 14.0 km
- (2.7 + 3.6 + 2.0 + 4.1 + 2.5) = 14.9 km
- (3.2 + 1.6 + 2.8 + 3.4 + 2.3) = 13.3 km

Total length:
$$\sum L_{i} = 14.5 + 12.4 + 14.0 + 14.9 + 13.3 = 69.1 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5.2 — Substitute into the density formula.**

$$D = \dfrac{69.1 \text{ km}}{100 \text{ km}^{2}} = 0.691 \text{ km/km}^{2}$$

**Step 5.3 — Interpretation.**
A density of **0.69 km/km²** is **moderate** (typical range 0.5 – 3.0), indicating a moderately fractured terrain with potential for groundwater accumulation along lineament intersections.

**Final answer.**
$$\boxed{D = 0.691 \text{ km/km}^{2}}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Azimuth (θ̄)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a simple arithmetic mean fails for circular (azimuthal) data**
> Lineament azimuths are *circular* data: a value of **5°** is only **10°** away from **355°**, not **350°**. The correct statistical treatment of circular data uses the **double-angle transformation** (Mardia & Jupp, 1999, *Directional Statistics* methodology, universally followed in structural geology).

**Step 1 — Convert each azimuth to its double angle $2\theta_{i}$.**

$$2\theta_{i} \text{ (degrees)} = 2 \times \theta_{i}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the sines and cosines of all double angles.**

$$X = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \cos 2\theta_{i}, \qquad Y = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sin 2\theta_{i}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the resultant vector length $R$.**

$$R = \sqrt{X^{2} + Y^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mean double angle.**

$$2\bar{\theta} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{X}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Recover the mean azimuth.**

$$\bar{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{X}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\sum \sin 2\theta_{i}}{\sum \cos 2\theta_{i}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quadrant correction**
> The $\arctan$ function returns a value in $(-\pi/2, +\pi/2)$. KTU students must apply the **quadrant correction rule**:
> - If $X > 0$ and $Y > 0$ → first quadrant → no change.
> - If $X < 0$ → add **180°** to the result.
> - If $X > 0$ and $Y < 0$ → add **360°** to keep the result in $(0°, 360°)$.
> - If both zero → data is uniformly distributed; mean azimuth is undefined.

**Step 6 — Worked example.**

> A small dataset has 4 lineaments with azimuths $\theta = \{ 30°, 50°, 200°, 220° \}$.

| $\theta_{i}$ | $2\theta_{i}$ | $\cos 2\theta_{i}$ | $\sin 2\theta_{i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 30° | 60° | +0.5000 | +0.8660 |
| 50° | 100° | −0.1736 | +0.9848 |
| 200° | 400° → 40° | +0.7660 | +0.6428 |
| 220° | 440° → 80° | +0.1736 | +0.9848 |

(Values of $2\theta$ are reduced modulo **360°**.)

**Step 6.1 — Sum the columns.**

$$\sum \cos 2\theta_{i} = 0.5000 - 0.1736 + 0.7660 + 0.1736 = 1.2660$$

$$\sum \sin 2\theta_{i} = 0.8660 + 0.9848 + 0.6428 + 0.9848 = 3.4784$$

**Step 6.2 — Apply the formula.**

$$2\bar{\theta} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{3.4784}{1.2660}\right) = \arctan(2.7474) = 70.0°$$

$$\bar{\theta} = 35.0°$$

**Final answer.**
$$\boxed{\bar{\theta} = 35.0°}$$

> The mean azimuth is **N 35° E**, confirming a NE-SW preferred orientation in this dataset.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation 3 — Circular Variance (S²)

**Step 1 — From the previous derivation, compute $R$ and $N$.**

$$R = \sqrt{X^{2} + Y^{2}} = \sqrt{1.2660^{2} + 3.4784^{2}} = \sqrt{1.6028 + 12.0992} = \sqrt{13.7020} = 3.7016$$

**Step 2 — Variance formula.**

$$S^{2} = 1 - \dfrac{R}{N} = 1 - \dfrac{3.7016}{4} = 1 - 0.9254 = 0.0746$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

| $S^{2}$ Value | Preferred Orientation? |
|---|---|
| $S^{2} \to 0$ | Strong preferred orientation (anisotropic) |
| $S^{2} \to 0.5$ | Moderate |
| $S^{2} \to 1$ | Random / isotropic distribution |

**Final answer.**
$$\boxed{S^{2} = 0.075 \text{ (strongly preferred orientation)}}$$

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation 4 — Lineament Frequency (F)

**Step 1 — Statement.**
Frequency is the *count* of lineaments per unit area.

$$F = \dfrac{N}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Worked example.**
With the previous 25-lineament example and $A = 100$ km²:

$$F = \dfrac{25}{100} = 0.25 \text{ count/km}^{2}$$

**Final answer.**
$$\boxed{F = 0.25 \text{ km}^{-2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Formulas to Engineering Problems

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix in the exam to instantly recognize which formula to deploy when a sub-question says...**

| Exam-Phrase Cue | Quantity to Compute | Formula | Working in Section |
|---|---|---|---|
| *"density of lineaments in the area"* | $D$ | $D = \dfrac{\sum L_{i}}{A}$ | 3.1.1 |
| *"number of lineaments per km²"* | $F$ | $F = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | 3.1.4 |
| *"average / mean direction of lineaments"* | $\bar{\theta}$ | $\bar{\theta} = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\big(\tfrac{\sum \sin 2\theta_{i}}{\sum \cos 2\theta_{i}}\big)$ | 3.1.2 |
| *"is the pattern preferred or random?"* | $S^{2}$ | $S^{2} = 1 - \tfrac{R}{N}$ | 3.1.3 |
| *"average length of lineaments"* | $\bar{L}$ | $\bar{L} = \tfrac{\sum L_{i}}{N}$ | (text) |
| *"intersection density / lineament crossing density"* | $I$ | $I = \tfrac{\text{intersections}}{A}$ | (text) |
| *"fracture trace vs. lineament — length criterion"* | classification | L ≥ 1.6 km → lineament | 2.4.3 |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Azimuth-to-Rose-Diagram Plotting Procedure (Practical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab / Practical Component (End-Semester Lab Exam will ask this)**

**Step 1.** Measure each lineament's azimuth from the map/imagery.

**Step 2.** Classify azimuths into **10° class intervals**: 0–10°, 10–20°, …, 350–360°.

**Step 3.** For each class, sum the **total length of lineaments** falling in that class (use **length-weighted** rose diagram — preferred for engineering applications).

**Step 4.** Compute the **petal length** (in cm or %) for each class.

**Step 5.** Plot petals on a **half-circle** or **full-circle** diagram. North (0°) is at the top; each petal is centred on its class midpoint.

**Step 6.** The **dominant petal** indicates the **preferred lineament trend**, which is the **principal stress direction (σ₁ or σ₃) projection on horizontal**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's tip:** A length-weighted rose diagram (sum of $L_i$ per 10° class) is preferred over a frequency rose (count of lineaments) because long lineaments have greater structural significance.

## 4.2 Schematic — Rose Diagram (Length-Weighted) Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Azimuth Class 0 to 10 deg]:::petal --> S[Sum Total Length in Class]:::op
    R2[Azimuth Class 10 to 20 deg]:::petal --> S
    R3[Azimuth Class 20 to 30 deg]:::petal --> S
    R4[Azimuth Class 30 to 40 deg]:::petal --> S
    R5[Azimuth Class ...]:::petal --> S
    R6[Azimuth Class 350 to 360 deg]:::petal --> S
    S --> N[Normalize to Percent]:::op
    N --> P[Plot Petal Length on 360 deg Diagram]:::plot
    P --> Q[Identify Dominant Trend Principal Stress Direction]:::plot

    classDef petal fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef plot fill:#fecaca,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Block-Flow Reading Guide:** Each 10° azimuth class (0–10°, 10–20°, …) has its lineament lengths summed, the totals are normalized, and the resulting petal lengths are plotted on a circular diagram whose dominant petal indicates the **principal horizontal stress direction**.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Cross-Cutting Relationships (Relative-Age Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Lineament A NE-SW Trending]:::l1 --> X3[Intersection Point]
    X2[Lineament B NW-SE Trending]:::l2 --> X3
    X3 --> Y{Displacement Visible?}:::decision
    Y -- Yes A displaces B --> Z1[B is Younger than A]:::conclusion
    Y -- Yes B displaces A --> Z2[A is Younger than B]:::conclusion
    Y -- No clear displacement --> Z3[Coeval or mutually offset]:::conclusion

    classDef l1 fill:#fb7185,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef l2 fill:#60a5fa,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#facc15,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef conclusion fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Block-Flow Reading Guide:** Two intersecting lineaments reveal their relative age only when one visibly displaces the other; the *displacing* lineament is younger. This is the **standard KTU cross-cutting criterion** for relative dating of lineaments and faults.

# Module 4 — Lineaments: Section 5

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pattern Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> - **Part A**: short-answer questions, each **3 marks**, no choice.
> - **Part B**: long-answer questions, each **14 marks**, with **internal choice between Q(a) and Q(b)** of identical CO/Bloom level.
> - The practice bank below mirrors this pattern exactly.

---

### 5.1.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *lineament* as used in structural geology. State the standard length criterion that distinguishes a lineament from a fracture trace.

**Model Answer (3 marks, ≈ 80–100 words).**

A **lineament** is a mappable, simple or composite linear surface feature whose parts are aligned in a straight or gently curving relationship and which differs distinctly from the patterns of adjacent features; it is interpreted to reflect a subsurface structural or lithological phenomenon (1.5 marks). The standard length criterion, following the Lattman (1958) convention used in Indian geological surveys, is that a linear surface feature **longer than 1.6 km** is classified as a lineament, while shorter features are termed **fracture traces** (1.5 marks).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** List any three genetic types of lineaments with one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks).**

1. **Structural / Tectonic lineament** — straight valleys or fault scarps marking fault traces; example: the Bhavani lineament along the Moyar–Bhavani shear zone, Tamil Nadu (1 mark).
2. **Lithological lineament** — ridges formed by differential erosion of resistant rock units such as dykes; example: quartz-vein ridges in the Aravalli belt (1 mark).
3. **Tonal / Spectral lineament** — straight colour-tone differences on multi-spectral satellite imagery related to vegetation stress or soil-moisture contrasts along fracture zones; example: NDVI anomalies along fractures in the Deccan traps (1 mark).

*(Alternatively, Geomorphological, Volcanic, or Anthropogenic types may be substituted.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) [7 marks]

**Schematic (use the Section 4.1 workflow diagram; describe each block in 1–2 sentences):**

**Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (1 mark).** Collect multi-spectral satellite imagery and ancillary data: **Landsat 8 OLI (30 m multispectral, 15 m panchromatic)** and **Sentinel-2 MSI (10 m visible, 20 m SWIR)** are two widely used free datasets; **SRTM DEM (30 m)** is added for hill-shade analysis; existing GSI 1:50,000 geological maps serve as the ground-truth reference.

**Stage 2 — Pre-Processing (1 mark).** Apply **radiometric calibration**, **FLAASH atmospheric correction**, **pan-sharpening** (e.g., Brovey transform) to enhance spatial detail, and **multi-azimuth hill-shade** generation (e.g., 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°) on the DEM to highlight subtle linear features.

**Stage 3 — Visual Interpretation (1 mark).** Interpret the imagery using standard **photo-interpretation keys** (tone, texture, pattern, shape, association, shadow). Use **edge-detection filters** — Sobel, Roberts, and Canny — to enhance the visibility of linear edges. Manually mark candidate lineaments on-screen.

**Stage 4 — Semi-Automatic Extraction (1.5 marks).** Apply automated algorithms to extract vector polylines:
- **PCI Geomatica LINE module** (commercial, industry standard).
- **Hough transform** (mathematical line-detection algorithm).
- **Fractalyse** (open-source for lineament analysis).
- **ArcGIS Lineament Extraction Tool** (raster-to-vector in GIS).
The output is a polyline shapefile with attributes (length, azimuth) for every extracted lineament.

**Stage 5 — Validation and Interpretation (1.5 marks).** Cross-check extracted lineaments with the **GSI geological map**, **gravity/magnetic anomaly maps**, and **field traverses**. Generate the final deliverables: a **lineament map**, a **rose diagram** of azimuths, a **density map** (kernel density in GIS), and a **tectonic interpretation** linking the dominant lineament trend to the **regional principal-stress direction**.

*(Examiner's note: a hand-drawn block diagram showing 5 boxes connected by arrows with 2 examples in each box earns full marks.)*

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the total lineament length $\sum L_{i}$ (1 mark).**

Summing all 40 lengths grouped by cluster:

- Cluster NE (rows 1–5, 11–13, 16–17, 19–20, 23–24, 26–27, 29, 31–32, 35–36, 38, 40): lengths = 3.2+2.8+4.1+1.9+2.5+3.0+2.6+4.5+1.7+3.3+2.0+3.7+1.6+2.4+1.5+2.7+1.9+2.1+3.5+2.4+3.1+1.7+2.2 = **56.3 km**.
- Cluster SW (rows 6–10, 14–15, 18, 21–22, 25, 28, 30, 33–34, 37, 39): lengths = 3.6+2.7+4.0+1.8+2.3+3.1+2.9+2.2+2.5+3.8+3.0+3.4+2.6+2.0+1.8+2.9+3.3 = **47.9 km**.

Total length:
$$\sum L_{i} = 56.3 + 47.9 = 104.2 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Compute lineament density $D$ (1 mark).**

$$D = \dfrac{\sum L_{i}}{A} = \dfrac{104.2 \text{ km}}{250 \text{ km}^{2}} = 0.4168 \text{ km/km}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sum \cos 2\theta_{i}$ and $\sum \sin 2\theta_{i}$ (2 marks).**

$$\sum \sin 2\theta_{i} = +35.8896$$

> (These sums are obtained by careful column addition; rounding to 4 decimal places is acceptable at KTU level.)

**Step 4 — Compute mean azimuth (1.5 marks).**

$$2\bar{\theta} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{35.8896}{9.9109}\right) = \arctan(3.6210) = 74.57°$$

(Here $X = \sum \cos 2\theta_{i} > 0$ and $Y = \sum \sin 2\theta_{i} > 0$, so the angle lies in the **first quadrant** and no quadrant correction is needed.)

$$\bar{\theta} = \dfrac{74.57°}{2} = 37.28°$$

**Step 5 — Compute circular variance (1 mark).**

$$R = \sqrt{X^{2} + Y^{2}} = \sqrt{9.9109^{2} + 35.8896^{2}} = \sqrt{98.226 + 1287.964} = \sqrt{1386.190} = 37.231$$

$$S^{2} = 1 - \dfrac{R}{N} = 1 - \dfrac{37.231}{40} = 1 - 0.9308 = 0.0692$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (0.5 mark).**

A circular variance of **S² = 0.069** is very close to 0, indicating a **strong preferred orientation** in the NE-SW / SW-NE direction. The mean azimuth of **37.3° (≈ N 37° E)** represents the principal preferred trend of the lineaments and likely reflects the principal horizontal stress direction (σ₁ or σ₃, depending on regional tectonic regime) acting on the bedrock.

**Final Answer Box.**

$$\boxed{D = 0.417 \text{ km/km}^{2}, \quad \bar{\theta} = 37.3°, \quad S^{2} = 0.069 \text{ (strong preferred orientation)}}$$
