> **[KTU University Exam — Model End-Semester Question, 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**
> **Question B.** *(Module 4, CO3, Bloom Level: Understand / Apply)*
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Classify lineaments on the basis of their **origin** with suitable examples. Discuss in detail the **engineering geological significance** of lineament analysis with reference to (i) dam site selection, (ii) tunnel alignment, and (iii) landslides in the Western Ghats.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A regional study area of **400 km²** contains 64 lineaments of total cumulative length **192 km**. Compute (i) the **lineament density (D)** and (ii) the **lineament frequency (F)**. If the regional stress field is inferred to be a strike-slip regime with the principal compression direction along **N 30° E**, what does the **mean azimuth** of the mapped lineaments (assumed to be **N 60° E**) imply about the structural grain of the terrain? Discuss, with the help of a neat block diagram, the relationship between **σ₁, σ₂, σ₃** and the **observed lineament trends**.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) [7 marks]

**Part 1 — Genetic classification of lineaments (3 marks).**

| # | Genetic Class | Defining Characteristic | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Structural / Tectonic** | Direct surface expression of joint, fault, shear zone, fold axis | Bhavani lineament, Tamil Nadu |
| 2 | **Lithological** | Differential erosion of dipping beds, dykes, sills, contrasting units | Quartz-reef ridges, Aravalli belt |
| 3 | **Geomorphological** | Aligned river valleys, escarpments, drainage anomalies | NNW-SSE rivers of Kerala |
| 4 | **Tonal / Spectral** | Straight colour-tone differences on multi-spectral imagery | NDVI anomalies, Deccan traps |
| 5 | **Volcanic / Igneous** | Surface traces of dyke swarms, ring dykes, volcanic rifts | Deccan dolerite dyke swarms |
| 6 | **Anthropogenic** | Man-made linear features mimicking structure | Canals, field boundaries |

(Any three well-explained classes earn 3 marks.)

**Part 2 — Engineering geological significance (4 marks).**

**(i) Dam site selection (1.5 marks).** Lineaments represent zones of bedrock weakness with high secondary permeability. Dams founded across major lineaments suffer from **seepage, piping, and base-failure** problems. Standard site-selection practice (as per IS 7321 and IS 14911) requires:
- Avoiding dam axes along **regional lineaments**;
- Treating lineament crossings in the **reservoir rim** by grouting to prevent leakage;
- Conducting **Lugeon tests** to quantify permeability along any intersecting lineament.

**(ii) Tunnel alignment (1.5 marks).** Tunnels driven perpendicular to dominant lineament sets experience **higher rock burst risk and squeezing ground**; parallel alignment may be more stable if the lineaments are sealed. Key considerations:
- Avoid portals at **lineament intersections** (highest stress concentration);
- Use **TBM (Tunnel Boring Machine)** if the lineament set is at high angle to the tunnel axis;
- Map **cataclastic zones** along lineaments to predict **over-break** and water inflow.

**(iii) Landslides in the Western Ghats (1 mark).** The Western Ghats have a well-documented NNW-SSE structural grain with laterite cap-rock underlain by deeply weathered gneiss/charnockite. Lineament-controlled joints form **kinematically viable failure planes** for:
- **Planar failure** along dip-slope lineaments;
- **Wedge failure** at lineament intersections;
- **Toppling failure** along steeply dipping lineaments.
The 2018 Kerala floods and 2019 Puttumala landslide (Wayanad) were directly correlated with lineament-controlled slope failure. Pre-monsoon lineament mapping using **high-resolution DEMs (CARTOSAT-3, 0.25 m)** is now mandatory for road and railway projects in the Ghats.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute lineament density (1.5 marks).**

$$D = \dfrac{\sum L_{i}}{A} = \dfrac{192 \text{ km}}{400 \text{ km}^{2}} = 0.48 \text{ km/km}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute lineament frequency (1.5 marks).**

$$F = \dfrac{N}{A} = \dfrac{64}{400 \text{ km}^{2}} = 0.16 \text{ count/km}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Tectonic interpretation (4 marks).**

The mean azimuth of the mapped lineaments is **N 60° E** (i.e., the dominant trend is ENE-WSW), while the principal compression direction (σ₁) is inferred to be **N 30° E**.

> **Relationship in a strike-slip regime:**
> In a strike-slip tectonic regime, two principal fault orientations are expected:
> - **Conjugate strike-slip faults** oriented at **~30° to σ₁** (Riedel shears).
> - **Reverse faults** oriented **perpendicular to σ₁**.
> - **Extension fractures (joints)** oriented **parallel to σ₁**.

Here σ₁ is **N 30° E**. The mapped lineaments trend **N 60° E**, i.e., they lie at an angle of **30° clockwise from σ₁**. This angular relationship is **diagnostic of Riedel (synthetic) shear fractures** in a strike-slip regime.

The σ₂ axis is **horizontal and oriented N 60° W** (perpendicular to σ₁ in the horizontal plane), and σ₃ is **horizontal, oriented N 60° E** — i.e., the **principal extension direction is parallel to the observed lineament trend**. This indicates that the lineaments have opened as **extension fractures and shear fractures** in a transpressional strike-slip regime.

**Step 4 — Block diagram representation (textual, since KTU accepts a hand-drawn sketch for 1 mark).**

A schematic block diagram should show:
- A horizontal σ₁ arrow pointing **N 30° E**.
- A horizontal σ₃ arrow pointing **N 60° E** (parallel to the lineament trend).
- A horizontal σ₂ arrow pointing **N 60° W** (perpendicular to both).
- Two sets of conjugate Riedel shears at **±30° to σ₁** (i.e., at **N 0° E** and **N 60° E** — the latter matches the observed mean azimuth).
- Extension fractures parallel to σ₁.
- A caption linking the **N 60° E** lineament set to the **Riedel R-shears** of the regional stress field.

**Final Answer Box.**

$$\boxed{D = 0.48 \text{ km/km}^{2}, \quad F = 0.16 \text{ km}^{-2}, \quad \text{Orientation: Riedel R-shear set in a strike-slip regime with } \sigma_{1} = N 30° E}$$

---

## 5.2 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 6 Ways KTU Students Lose Marks in Lineament Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the length criterion (1.6 km)**: When asked the difference between a *fracture trace* and a *lineament*, students often give vague answers. **Always state the 1.6 km cut-off explicitly** (after Lattman, 1958 — universally accepted in Indian engineering-geology practice).
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean for azimuth**: The simple arithmetic mean of azimuths is **statistically invalid** for circular data because 5° and 355° differ by 10°, not 350°. **Always use the double-angle (Mardia) method**.
> 3. **Skipping the quadrant correction**: The `arctan` function returns a value in $(-\pi/2, +\pi/2)$. If you compute $2\bar{\theta}$ and get a negative number, you **must add 360°** before dividing by 2. Failure to do so is the single most common numerical error in KTU answer sheets.
> 4. **Wrong units in lineament density**: Density is **km of lineament per km² of area**, not "lineaments per km²". The latter is the **frequency**. Examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 5. **Confusing σ₁ with σ₃ in lineament interpretation**: In an **extensional regime**, σ₃ controls the opening direction and **lineaments are parallel to σ₃**, not σ₁. In a **compressional regime**, lineaments often follow σ₂. **Always state the regime explicitly** before naming the stress axis.
> 6. **Plotting the rose diagram with a north arrow on the side**: The **0° petal (North) must be at the top** of the rose diagram and **petals are centred on class midpoints** (e.g., 5°, 15°, 25°). Plotting petals at the class boundaries (0°, 10°, 20°) will offset every petal by 5° and lose you 1 mark.
> 7. **Ignoring anthropogenic lineaments**: When given a real satellite image, students often include roads, canals, and field boundaries as lineaments. **These must be filtered out** during structural mapping — examiners will deduct 1 mark per misinterpretation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Scan**
> Memorize this checklist before every KTU Module-4 attempt.

**Core Definitions**

- ☐ **Lineament** = mappable, simple/composite, straight-to-curving surface feature reflecting a subsurface structure.
- ☐ **Fracture trace** = natural linear surface feature **< 1.6 km** long.
- ☐ **Lineament** = natural linear surface feature **≥ 1.6 km** long.
- ☐ **Photolineament** = lineament identified **only** on photos/imagery.
- ☐ **Megalineament** = lineament **> 100 km** long (regional/continental scale).

**Genetic Classes (six standard)**

- ☐ Structural, Lithological, Geomorphological, Tonal/Spectral, Volcanic, Anthropogenic.

**Five-Stage Workflow**

- ☐ Acquisition → Pre-processing → Visual Interpretation → Semi-Automatic Extraction → Validation/Interpretation.

**Key Formulas (with units)**

- ☐ $D = \sum L_{i} / A$ (km/km²)
- ☐ $F = N / A$ (count/km²)
- ☐ $\bar{\theta} = \tfrac{1}{2}\arctan\!\left(\tfrac{\sum \sin 2\theta_{i}}{\sum \cos 2\theta_{i}}\right)$ (degrees)
- ☐ $R = \sqrt{(\sum \sin 2\theta_{i})^{2} + (\sum \cos 2\theta_{i})^{2}}$
- ☐ $S^{2} = 1 - R/N$ (0 = preferred, 1 = isotropic)

**Engineering & Tectonic Significance**

- ☐ **Dam sites**: avoid lineament crossings in the foundation.
- ☐ **Tunnels**: avoid portals at lineament intersections; align to minimize stress concentration.
- ☐ **Landslides (Western Ghats)**: lineaments form planar / wedge / toppling failure planes.
- ☐ **Hydrogeology**: lineaments = secondary porosity and groundwater conduits in hard-rock aquifers.
- ☐ **Seismic hazard**: active lineaments may be capable faults (AERB norms).
- ☐ **Mineral / hydrocarbon exploration**: ore bodies and traps cluster at lineament intersections.

**Cross-Cutting & Relative-Age Rules**

- ☐ The lineament that **displaces** the other is **younger**.
- ☐ Anthropogenic lineaments (roads, canals) must be **filtered out**.

**Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid**

- ☐ Always reduce $2\theta$ modulo **360°** before computing sine and cosine.
- ☐ Always apply **quadrant correction** to `arctan`.
- ☐ Use **length-weighted** rose diagrams (sum of $L_i$ per class) — not frequency rose.
- ☐ Standard rose-diagram class interval = **10°** (regional: 30°).
- ☐ Standard satellite data: **Landsat 30 m / Sentinel-2 10 m / SRTM DEM 30 m / ASTER 15 m / CARTOSAT 2.5 m**.
- ☐ Standard geological map scale in KTU problems = **1:50,000 (GSI Quadrangle)**.

**Stress-Regime Quick Reference**

| Regime | σ₁ | σ₃ | Expected Lineament Trends |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Extensional** | Vertical | Horizontal | Parallel to σ₃ (extensional joints) |
| **Compressional** | Horizontal | Vertical | Perpendicular to σ₁ (or Riedel shears at ±30° to σ₁) |
| **Strike-slip** | Horizontal | Horizontal | Riedel shears at ±30° to σ₁; extension fractures parallel to σ₁ |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Lineaments in Kerala and Its Possible Implications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET615 - STRUCTURAL GEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Major Lineaments in Kerala and Its Possible Implications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **lineament** is a mappable, linear or curvilinear surficial feature of the Earth's crust, whose parts are aligned in a straight or gently curved pattern and is presumed to reflect a **zone of structural weakness** (fault, shear zone, fracture, fold axis, or lithological contact) in the underlying bedrock. The term was popularized by **W. H. Hobbs (1904, 1912)** and is now integral to neotectonic and remote-sensing-based structural analysis.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, lineaments are studied as surface expressions of **deep-seated crustal discontinuities** that control drainage patterns, seismicity, and geomorphic evolution of a region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> *Major Lineaments in Kerala and Its Possible Implications* — Students must identify the principal lineaments, describe their **trend, length, tectonic setting**, and discuss their role in **seismotectonics, geomorphology, landslides, hydrogeology, and engineering projects (dams, tunnels, bridges)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a glass sheet. If you apply pressure from the sides, it does not break in a random, curved way — it fractures along **straight lines** that follow zones where the glass is weakest. The Earth's crust behaves the same way: when tectonic stresses act over millions of years, the brittle upper crust **fractures along preferred linear zones**. These fracture zones, when revealed by rivers, vegetation contrast, or topography, are **lineaments**.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of lineaments as the **"scars" or "stretch marks"** of the Earth. They are linear features that record ancient and ongoing tectonic stresses. The longer, deeper, and more active a lineament is, the more it influences the landscape, water flow, and earthquake risk of a region.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Lineament length threshold (Kerala studies):** Generally features longer than **1.6 km** are considered significant lineaments; however, regional shear zones like the **Achankovil Shear Zone (>120 km long)** are mapped at crustal scale.
- **Typical width of a lineament zone:** From a few metres (single fracture) to **>5 km** (broad shear corridors like Palghat).
- **Resolution of remote sensing data:** Landsat (30 m), Sentinel-2 (10 m), Cartosat DEM (10 m) — used for lineament extraction in Kerala.
- **Major trends identified in Kerala (azimuth):** **NNW–SSE to NW–SE** (dominant), **NE–SW**, and **N–S to NNE–SSW**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework
A lineament is not a single fault but a **linear structural domain** comprising multiple parallel/sub-parallel faults, joints, and shear planes. The structural interpretation of a lineament involves:

- **Geometric Analysis** – Trend (azimuth), length, spacing, density.
- **Kinematic Analysis** – Sense of displacement (strike-slip, dip-slip, oblique).
- **Dynamic Analysis** – Stress field orientation that produced it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are Kerala's lineaments important?**
> Kerala lies at the **southwestern tip of the Indian Peninsula**, a stable cratonic block bounded by the **Arabian Sea (west)**, the **Western Ghats escarpment (east)**, and the **Palghat–Cauvery Shear Zone (south of the Nilgiri block)**. The state records a mosaic of Precambrian crystalline rocks, Proterozoic shear zones, and passive-margin sediments, all cut by **NNW–SSE trending faults** that re-activate under the present NNE–SSW compressive stress field of the Indian plate.

### 2.2 The Major Lineaments of Kerala — Engineering Cheat Sheet

| # | Lineament | Trend (Azimuth) | Approx. Length | Tectonic Character | Key Field Evidence |
|---|-----------|----------------|----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Palghat (Palakkad) Gap Lineament** | NNW–SSE to N–S | ~150 km | Major rift / graben-like corridor | Cauvery & Bharathapuzha drainage divide; Bouguer gravity low |
| 2 | **Achankovil Shear Zone (AKSZ)** | NNW–SSE | ~120 km (within Kerala) | Proterozoic ductile shear reactivated as brittle strike-slip | Sheared gneisses, mylonites, hot springs at **Thriuvananthapuram** |
| 3 | **Moyar–Bhavani Lineament** | E–W to ENE–WSW | Crosses northern Kerala border | Craton-scale shear bounding the Nilgiri block | Linear valleys, shocked quartz, pseudotachylite |
| 4 | **Peringamala Lineament** | NW–SE | ~40 km | Brittle fault cutting the AKSZ | Hot springs, linear stream control |
| 5 | **Ponmudi Lineament** | NW–SE | ~30 km | Fault scarp and ridge offset | Offset drainage, breccia zones |
| 6 | **Idamalayar Lineament** | NNW–SSE | ~50 km | Fault in the Western Ghats | Controls Idukki reservoir alignment |
| 7 | **Periyar Lineament** | NW–SE | ~70 km | Fault along the Periyar river course | Linear valley, springs, landslides |
| 8 | **Changanasseri–Kanjirapuzha Lineament** | NNW–SSE | ~60 km | Hidden basement fault in mid-Kerala | Lineament density peak, geomorphic anomalies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam — "PAPA-PM-MP-CPI"**
> **P**alghat • **A**chankovil • **P**eriyar • **A**chankovil/Tenkasi
> **P**eringamala • **M**oyar
> **P**onmudi • **I**damalayar
> **C**hanganasseri

### 2.3 Geological Setting
Kerala's lineaments are predominantly **reactivated Precambrian structures** that owe their origin to:
- The **Gondwana suturing event (~550 Ma)** — produced NNW–SSE shear zones.
- The **Deccan volcanism (~65 Ma)** — reactivated fractures along the Western Ghats margin.
- The **Himalayan orogeny (post-50 Ma)** — created the present NNE–SSW compressive regime that reactivates older lineaments.
- **Neotectonic activity** — continues to produce minor seismic events (e.g., 2000 Idukki earthquake M 4.5, 2019 Wadakkanchery).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Dam site selection** — Idukki, Idamalayar, Mullaperiyar, Sholayar dams all lie on or near lineaments; their structural stability depends on the integrity of these zones.
- **Tunnel alignment** — Tail-race tunnels of Idukki hydro-electric project cross the **Idamalayar Lineament**.
- **Seismic hazard zonation** — Kerala falls in **Zone II/III** of IS 1893; lineament-density maps upgrade the hazard category locally.
- **Groundwater targeting** — Lineament intersections are high-yield borehole zones.
- **Landslide risk** — Steep lineament-controlled slopes in the Western Ghats are the primary landslide corridors during monsoons (e.g., 2018 Kavalappara, 2019 Puthumala).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework

### 3.1 Lineament Analysis Workflow (KTU Practical/ESE Standard)

**Step 1 — Data acquisition**
- Multispectral imagery (Landsat 8 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI).
- DEM (Cartosat-1, SRTM 30 m) for hill-shade generation.
- Published geological maps of the **Geological Survey of India (GSI)**.

**Step 2 — Image processing**
- Band combinations: **7-5-3 (Landsat 8)** for lithological contrast.
- Hill-shade: Sun azimuth **315°**, sun elevation **45°** — to highlight linear ridges/valleys.
- Edge detection: Sobel/Laplacian filters enhance fractures.

**Step 3 — Lineament extraction**
- Manual digitization on-screen at scale **1:50,000** or **1:250,000**.
- Rose-diagram statistical analysis of trends.

**Step 4 — Calculation of lineament density (LD)**

$$
LD = \frac{\sum L_i}{A}
$$

where:
- $LD$ = Lineament density (km/km²)
- $\sum L_i$ = Total length of all lineaments in the area (km)
- $A$ = Area of the study window (km²)

> **Boundary condition:** For Kerala's mid-land, typical $LD$ ranges from **0.2 to 1.8 km/km²**. Higher density (>2.0 km/km²) is associated with the **Palghat Gap and Achankovil Shear Zone corridors**.

**Step 5 — Calculation of lineament frequency (LF)**

$$
LF = \frac{N}{A}
$$

where $N$ is the total number of lineaments. This separates *many short fractures* from *few long shears*.

**Step 6 — Tectonic interpretation**
- Compare lineament trends with the present-day stress field.
- Use **bifurcation ratio** of streams and **drainage anomalies** (sharp bends, rectangular patterns) as proxies for active faulting.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Estimating Seismic Hazard from Lineament Density

> **Problem (KTU 2019 pattern):**
> A 100 km² area in the Western Ghats shows 45 lineaments whose total length is 120 km. Calculate the lineament density and comment on the relative seismic hazard.

**Solution:**

$$
LD = \frac{\sum L_i}{A} = \frac{120 \text{ km}}{100 \text{ km}^2} = 1.2 \text{ km/km}^2
$$

$$
LF = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{45}{100 \text{ km}^2} = 0.45 \text{ per km}^2
$$

**Interpretation (according to standard KTU reference values):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LD} \le 0.5\ \text{km/km}^2 &\rightarrow \text{Low structural disturbance (stable zone)} \\
0.5 < \text{LD} \le 1.0\ \text{km/km}^2 &\rightarrow \text{Moderate disturbance} \\
\text{LD} > 1.0\ \text{km/km}^2 &\rightarrow \text{High disturbance / potential seismic risk}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **1.2 km/km² > 1.0 km/km²**, the area is classified as a **high lineament-density zone**, suggesting **moderate to high neotectonic activity** and an elevated seismic hazard category.

### 3.3 Derivation of Stress Orientation from Lineament Trends

For a strike-slip regime, two conjugate sets of faults form at angles of $\pm 30°$ to the principal compressive stress $\sigma_1$ (Anderson's theory of faulting).

$$
\theta_{\sigma_1} = \frac{\theta_{f1} + \theta_{f2}}{2}
$$

where:
- $\theta_{\sigma_1}$ = Azimuth of maximum compressive stress
- $\theta_{f1}, \theta_{f2}$ = Azimuths of the two conjugate fault/lineament sets

**Application to Kerala:** Two prominent trends observed:
- Set 1: **NNW–SSE (azimuth 340°)** — Achankovil-type shears
- Set 2: **NE–SW (azimuth 050°)** — cross-faults

$$
\theta_{\sigma_1} = \frac{340° + 50°}{2} = \boxed{015°\ \text{(NNE–SSW)}}
$$

This matches the **GPS-derived present-day India-plate compressive stress direction (~N15°E)** — confirming that Kerala's lineaments are **reactivated under the present-day stress field**, a key KTU concept.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Tectonic Architecture of Kerala's Lineaments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Indian Plate Compressive Stress NNE-SSW"] --> A1["Stress Reactivation of Precambrian Shear Zones"]
    A1 --> B1["Palghat Gap Lineament NNW-SSE"]
    A1 --> B2["Achankovil Shear Zone NNW-SSE"]
    A1 --> B3["Periyar Lineament NW-SE"]
    A1 --> B4["Moyar-Bhavani Lineament EW"]
    A1 --> B5["Peringamala Lineament NW-SE"]
    A1 --> B6["Idamalayar Lineament NNW-SSE"]
    B1 --> C1["Drainage Diverted Bharathapuzha vs Cauvery"]
    B2 --> C2["Mylonite Shear Hot Springs at TVM"]
    B3 --> C3["Linear Valley along Periyar R"]
    B4 --> C4["Bounding Fault of Nilgiri Block"]
    B5 --> C5["Hot Springs at Peringamala"]
    B6 --> C6["Idukki Reservoir Alignment"]
    C1 --> D1["Geomorphic Implication"]
    C2 --> D2["Neotectonic Activity"]
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C5 --> D2
    C6 --> D3["Engineering Dam Site Concern"]
    D1 --> E1["Seismic Hazard Zone III Local Upgrade"]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E2["Dam Stability and Tunnel Support"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Lineament Implications Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "CAUSES"
        K1["Gondwana Tectonics 550 Ma"]
        K2["Deccan Volcanism 65 Ma"]
        K3["Himalayan Compression Present Day"]
    end
    subgraph "LINEAMENTS IN KERALA"
        L1["Palghat Gap"]
        L2["Achankovil Shear"]

    end

        M1["Seismotectonics"]
        M2["Geomorphology"]
        M3["Hydrogeology"]
        M4["Landslides"]
        M5["Mineralization"]
        M6["Dam and Tunnel Engineering"]
    end
    K1 --> L1
    K1 --> L2
    K2 --> L3
    K2 --> L4
    K3 --> L1
    K3 --> L2
    K3 --> L5
    K3 --> L6

    L1 --> M2
    L2 --> M1
    L2 --> M5
    L3 --> M1
    L4 --> M2
    L4 --> M4
    L5 --> M1
    L5 --> M3
    L6 --> M4
    L6 --> M6
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Rose Diagram Schematic (Lineament Trends)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0["Rose Diagram Schematic Kerala Lineaments"]
    R0 --> R1["NNW-SSE 340 deg DOMINANT 42 percent"]
    R0 --> R2["NW-SE 305 deg 23 percent"]
    R0 --> R3["NE-SW 050 deg 18 percent"]
    R0 --> R4["N-S to NNE 010 deg 12 percent"]
    R0 --> R5["E-W 090 deg 5 percent"]
    R1 --> I1["Achankovil Palghat Idamalayar"]
    R2 --> I2["Periyar Peringamala Ponmudi"]
    R3 --> I3["Cross Faults of Western Ghats"]
    R4 --> I4["Recent Reactivation Sets"]
    R5 --> I5["Moyar Bhavani Boundary"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The dominance of the **NNW–SSE trend (≈ 340°)** is a direct reflection of the **Panafrican / Gondwana stress fabric**, while the secondary **NE–SW trend** represents the **Himalayan-age conjugate set**. Together they form a **conjugate pair indicative of active strike-slip tectonics**.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Engineering Implications Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Lineament Zone Identification"] --> P1["Surface Mapping and Remote Sensing"]
    P1 --> P2["DEM and Hill-shade Analysis"]
    P2 --> P3["Rose Diagram and Density Maps"]
    P3 --> P4["Engineering Decisions"]
    P4 --> P41["Dam Foundation Grouting"]
    P4 --> P42["Tunnel Route Alignment"]
    P4 --> P43["Bridge Abutment Siting"]
    P4 --> P44["Borehole Location for Groundwater"]
    P4 --> P45["Landslide Mitigation Measures"]
    P4 --> P46["Seismic Microzonation Update"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define a lineament. Mention any two major lineaments of Kerala.*

**Model Answer:**
A lineament is a mappable, linear or curvilinear surface feature of the Earth's crust that is believed to reflect a **zone of structural weakness** in the underlying bedrock (fault, fracture, shear zone, fold axis or lithological contact). It is identified using remote-sensing imagery, DEMs and geomorphic criteria.
**Two major lineaments of Kerala:**
1. **Palghat (Palakkad) Gap Lineament** — NNW–SSE trending, controls the divide between the Cauvery and Bharathapuzha basins.
2. **Achankovil Shear Zone** — NNW–SSE Proterozoic ductile shear reactivated as a brittle strike-slip fault, with associated hot springs near Thiruvananthapuram.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is the tectonic significance of the Palghat Gap?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Palghat Gap** is a major **NNW–SSE trending structural corridor** (~150 km long) that cuts across the Western Ghats between the Nilgiri Hills and the Anaimalai range. It is interpreted as a **rift-related graben / fault-bounded corridor** that separates the **Madurai Block (south)** from the **Nilgiri–Salem Block (north)**. Tectonically, it (i) acts as a drainage divide between the eastward-flowing Cauvery system and the westward-flowing Bharathapuzha system, (ii) shows a pronounced **Bouguer gravity low** confirming a deep crustal root, and (iii) is a **seismically sensitive zone** that has recorded low-to-moderate earthquakes in historical times.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Module 4 pattern]`

**(a)** Describe the **Achankovil Shear Zone** with reference to its location, trend, length, rock types affected, and geomorphic signatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **possible implications of major lineaments in Kerala** with respect to seismotectonics, landslides, hydrogeology, and engineering structures. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

The **Achankovil Shear Zone (AKSZ)** is one of the longest and most prominent structural lineaments in southern India, extending from **Achankovil in Kerala** through **Tenkasi and Courtallam in Tamil Nadu** for a total length of **>120 km within Kerala** and continuing further south. Key features:

- **Trend:** NNW–SSE (azimuth ~ 340°), steeply dipping.
- **Width:** Variable from 1 to 5 km.
- **Rock types affected:** **Charnockites, khondalites, gneisses and migmatites** of the **Trivandrum Block** and **Madurai Block**. The shear separates the high-grade Trivandrum granulites from the Madurai Block gneisses.
- **Geomorphic signatures:**
  1. Linear alignment of **hot springs** at **Thiruvananthapuram (Varkala, Vattiyoorkavu)** — indicating deep circulation along a permeable fault.
  2. **Drainage anomalies** — the **Kallada river** and the **Achankovil river** show parallel, straight courses controlled by the shear fabric.
  3. **Saddle-and-vee topography** along the western flank of the Western Ghats.
  4. **Mylonite and ultramylonite bands** mapped at the surface and confirmed in quarry sections.
- **Tectonic character:** Originally a **Proterozoic ductile thrust** that was **reactivated in the late Cretaceous–Tertiary** as a **brittle strike-slip / oblique-slip fault** under the present NNE–SSW compressive stress field.

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**1. Seismotectonic implications:** The **Palghat Gap, Achankovil Shear Zone, Periyar Lineament and Idamalayar Lineament** are potential loci for **future moderate earthquakes** because they are aligned with the present-day **NNE–SSW compressive stress**. The 2000 Idukki earthquake (M 4.5) and 2019 Wadakkanchery event (M 3.4) are evidence of reactivation. The Bureau of Indian Standards places Kerala in **Zone II/III**; locally, lineament-density maps upgrade the hazard to **Zone III**.

**2. Landslide implications:** The **Western Ghats scarp** is dissected by **NW–SE and NNW–SSE lineaments** that create **highly fractured, weak rock masses** with steep slopes. During the **2018 (Kavalappara, Puthumala) and 2019 (Munnar, Kottayam)** catastrophic landslides, the failure zones were found to coincide with lineament corridors. Lineament-controlled valleys act as **debris-flow channels**.

**3. Hydrogeological implications:** **Lineament intersections** in the hard-rock terrain of Kerala (crystalline gneisses, granulites) are the **most productive aquifer targets**, with yields of **5–20 L/s** compared to the regional average of **0.5–2 L/s**. The **Peringamala and Idamalayar lineaments** control spring discharge and baseflow of several westward rivers.

**4. Engineering-structure implications:**
- **Dams:** Idukki (Idamalayar lineament), Mullaperiyar, Sholayar and Kakki dams are located on or near lineaments, requiring **deep curtain grouting** to seal fractured foundations.
- **Tunnels:** The **Idukki tail-race tunnel** crosses the Idamalayar lineament — rock bursts and heavy seepage were encountered.
- **Bridges and highways:** The **Kochi–Dhanushkodi NH-85** alignment crosses the Achankovil Shear Zone near Kottarakara, demanding **flexible pavement design** to accommodate differential settlement.
- **Seismic microzonation:** Cities such as **Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram and Palakkad** are advised to incorporate lineament corridors in their **disaster-management plans** under the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) guidelines.

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **Moyar–Bhavani Lineament** and its role in the tectonic framework of south India. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches/diagrams, describe the **methodology of lineament analysis from satellite imagery and DEMs**. How is lineament density used in seismic hazard assessment? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

The **Moyar–Bhavani Lineament** is a **craton-scale, ENE–WSW to E–W trending shear** that runs along the **northern boundary of the Nilgiri Hills**, separating the **Nilgiri Block (north)** from the **Madurai Block (south)**. Key features:

- **Length:** Extends for **>250 km** from the Western Ghats scarp to the **Eastern Ghats near Salem**.
- **Tectonic character:** Originally a **Proterozoic ductile shear**, reactivated as a **brittle normal / strike-slip fault**. It is a **fundamental crustal boundary** that juxtaposes two distinct granulite-facies terranes.
- **Significance in the tectonic framework of south India:**
  1. It represents the **southern limit of the Dharwar Craton's exposed Archaean nucleus**.
  2. It marks the **suture-like boundary** between the older (>2.5 Ga) Nilgiri granulites and the younger Proterozoic Madurai Block.
  3. The **Moyar river** and **Bhavani river** flow along the shear, providing classic **lithologically controlled drainage**.
  4. The shear is a **gravity anomaly boundary** separating high-Bouguer Nilgiri plateau from the low-Bouguer Bhavani basin.
  5. It is considered a **potential source of moderate seismic events**, although historical seismicity is low.

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Methodology of lineament analysis:**

**Step 1 — Data acquisition:**
- Optical data: **Landsat 8 OLI (30 m)**, **Sentinel-2 MSI (10 m)**.
- DEM: **SRTM (30 m)**, **Cartosat-1 (10 m)**, **ALOS PALSAR (12.5 m)**.

**Step 2 — Pre-processing:**
- Pan-sharpening of multispectral images.
- Generation of **hill-shade relief** with multiple sun azimuths (45°, 135°, 225°, 315°).

**Step 3 — Lineament extraction:**
- **Visual interpretation** by an experienced geologist — the gold standard.
- **Semi-automatic extraction** using PCI Geomatica LINE module.
- **Edge-detection filters** (Sobel, Laplacian) for enhancement.

**Step 4 — Field validation:**
- Ground-truth mapping of shear zones, breccias, slickensides.

**Step 5 — Statistical analysis:**
- **Rose diagrams** for trend analysis.
- **Lineament-density (LD)** and **lineament-frequency (LF)** maps.

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**
- Overlap with **seismic epicentres**, **landslide inventory**, **spring locations**, and **dam foundations**.

**Use in seismic hazard assessment:**
Lineament density is directly correlated with **fracture density** and hence with **potential slip surface availability**. KTU/GSI standard classification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LD < 0.5\ \text{km/km}^2 &\rightarrow \text{Stable zone} \\
0.5 \le LD < 1.0\ \text{km/km}^2 &\rightarrow \text{Moderate hazard} \\
LD \ge 1.0\ \text{km/km}^2 &\rightarrow \text{High hazard / seismic source zone}
\end{aligned}
$$

Areas of high LD and intersection of two or more lineament trends are designated as **Seismic Source Zones (SSZ)** in microzonation studies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Do not list lineaments without their trend and tectonic character** — naming alone gets only 1 of 7 marks. Always add **trend, length, rock types and tectonic significance**.
> 2. **Avoid confusing "joint" and "fault" with "lineament".** A lineament is a **regional-scale linear feature**, not a single joint. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Engineering implications must be specific to Kerala** — mentioning generic dam problems loses marks. Refer to **Idukki, Mullaperiyar, Idamalayar** by name.
> 4. **For numerical problems**, always show the **formula, substitution and unit** clearly. A bare answer loses half the marks.
> 5. **Do not write about the Himalayas** when asked about Kerala — stay focused on **Peninsular Indian cratonic** tectonics.
> 6. **In rose diagrams**, always label the **azimuth and percentage** of each trend. Examiners expect at least **three dominant trends** to be identified.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A lineament is a **mappable linear/curvilinear surface feature** reflecting a **crustal zone of weakness**.
- **Dominant trend in Kerala:** **NNW–SSE (340°)**, with secondary **NW–SE (305°)**, **NE–SW (050°)**, **NNE–SSW (010°)** and **E–W (090°)**.
- **Eight major lineaments to remember:**
  1. **Palghat (Palakkad) Gap** — NNW–SSE, rift-like, drainage divide.
  2. **Achankovil Shear Zone** — NNW–SSE, Proterozoic, hot springs.
  3. **Moyar–Bhavani** — ENE–WSW, craton boundary, Nilgiri.
  4. **Peringamala** — NW–SE, hot springs.
  5. **Ponmudi** — NW–SE, ridge offset.
  6. **Idamalayar** — NNW–SSE, Idukki dam.
  7. **Periyar** — NW–SE, Periyar river control.
  8. **Changanasseri–Kanjirapuzha** — NNW–SSE, mid-Kerala basement fault.
- **Origin:** **Gondwana (550 Ma) + Deccan (65 Ma) + Himalayan reactivation (Present NNE–SSW compression).**
- **Key formula:** $LD = \sum L_i / A$ (km/km²). Threshold for high hazard = **> 1.0 km/km²**.
- **Stress derivation:** Two conjugate lineament sets give $\sigma_1$ at the **bisector** — for Kerala this is **N15°E**.
- **Implications — S-E-L-E-M-D** mnemonic:
  - **S**eismotectonic upgrade of Zone II/III,
  - **E**ngineering (dams, tunnels — Idukki, Mullaperiyar),
  - **L**andslides (2018, 2019 Western Ghats events),
  - **E**rosion and linear valleys,
  - **M**ineralization (shear-hosted gold and REE in AKSZ),
  - **D**rainage anomalies.
- **Examiners love:** (i) the trend list, (ii) the Idukki / Achankovil / Palghat trio, (iii) the lineament density formula, (iv) the present-day stress derivation.
- **Avoid:** Confusing lineaments with joints; using European/Himalayan examples; listing without trend or length.
